Black.comments: Fields should be checked for aphid populations periodically after emergence.
Black.management: Sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use; in commercial plantations aphid numbers are usually kept in check by predators and natural enemies; beneficial insect populations should be assessed before chemical control is considered; if no beneficial insect populations are present and aphids are damaging then apply appropriate insecticides.

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Disease may be seed-borne.
Black.management: Use disease free seed; treat seeds prior to planting; rotate crops every 2 years.

Black.comments: Symptoms may not appear until the vines are mature with ripening fruit.
Black.management: Fumigate soil prior to planting; remove and destroy plant roots immediately following harvest.

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed.
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Black.disease: Cucumber beetles (Western striped cucumber beetle, Western spotted cucumber beetle, Banded cucumber beetle)
Black.comments: Beetles overwinter in soil and leaf litter and emerge from soil when temperatures begin to reach and exceed 12.7°C (55°F)
Black.management: Monitor new planting regularly for signs of beetle; floating row covers can be used to protect the plants from damage but will need to be removed at bloom to allow bees to pollinate plants; applications of kaolin clay can be effective for management of small beetle populations; application of appropriate insecticides may be necessary

Black.comments: Squash bugs overwinter in crop debris or under rocks and stones.
Black.management: Destroy all crops residue as soon as possible after harvest or on plant death; apply row covers at planting; apply insecticidal soap or appropriate insecticide.

Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Fungi can survive in soil for 8-10 years
Black.management: Disease significantly reduced by application of fungicides immediately after thinning plants; plow soil deeply; rotate crops with non-hosts

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors humid weather conditions; disease can be spread over long distance by wind movements
Black.management: Disease can be controlled by application of sulfur at first sign of symptoms, as long as temperatures are high enough

Black.disease: Slugs & snails (Gray garden slug, Spotted garden slug, Brown garden snail, European garden snail , etc.)
Black.comments: Slugs and snails prefer moist, shaded habitats and will shelter in weeds or organic trash; adults may deposit eggs in the soil throughout the season; damage to plants can be extensive
Black.management: Practice good garden sanitation by removing garden trash, weeds and plant debris to promote good air circulation and reduce moist habitat for slugs and snails; handpick slugs at night to decrease population; spread wood ashes or eggshells around plants; attract molluscs by leaving out organic matter such as lettuce or grapefruit skins, destroy any found feeding on lure; sink shallow dishes filled with beer into the soil to attract and drown the molluscs; chemical controls include ferrous phosphate for organic gardens and metaldehyde (e.g. Buggeta)  and carbaryl (e.g Sevin bait) for non-organic growers

Black.comments: Disease emergence occurs when high temperatures coincide with periods of high humidity
Black.management: Avoid planting millet varieties that are highly susceptible to Cercospora fungi; control weeds in field; rotate crops and practice good sanitation; no chemical control is needed to treat the disease

Black.comments: Some pecan varieties are susceptible to powdery mildew and disease is not controlled by scab fungicides and plants should be scouted for the disease over the summer months
Black.management: Trees showing symptoms should be treated with appropriate sulfur-containing fungicides

Black.comments: Fungus survives in plant debris on the ground
Black.management: Fungicide applications made to control scab should also be sufficient to control vein spot; there are some pecan varieties that are less susceptible to vein spot such as Melrose and Cherokee

Black.comments: Fungus overwinters in tissues infected previous year
Black.management: Scab is usually controlled by a series of fungicide sprays between budbreak and shell hardening

Black.comments: The characteristic stickiness of plants which is usually present during aphid infestation cannot be used as an indicator of black pecan aphid attack as this aphid does not produce honeydew
Black.management: Trees should be inspected for aphids regularly due to their potential to cause severe damage; an appropriate insecticide which does not harm beneficial insects should be applied to reduce the aphid population

Black.comments: Larvae pupate to adult moths within the nut; insect overwinters as larva and may undergo several generations per year with the total number determined by location
Black.management: The primary method of controlling the pecan nut casebearer is through the application of an appropriate insecticide; insect growth regulators are preferable to organophosphates, carbamates or pyrethroids which disrupt populations of beneficial insects;

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors warm, wet conditions
Black.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present

Black.comments: Fungi enter the trunk through wounds.
Black.management: Avoid wounding palms with machinery and tools to reduce disease incidence; disease can be controlled with applications of the fungicide benomyl where registered; infected trees should be removed and destroyed as soon as possible.

Black.comments: May be transmitted by leaf hoppers.
Black.management: The most effective method of managing the disease is to plant resistant coconut varieties such as Malayan dwarf or Maypan; antibiotic treatment is effective but not usually practical for large scale plantings.

Black.comments: Beetles are nocturnal and fly at night; also a damaging pest of oil palm.
Black.management: Destroy any decaying logs in plantation by chopping and burning to kill any larvae that may be inside; remove any dead trees from plantation and destroy by burning; plant a cover crop to deter egg laying by females as they do not lay eggs in areas covered by vegetation; hooked wire can be used to extract larvae that are boring into young crowns.

Black.comments: Primarily a disease of setts; fungus enters cut ends of stem or through other wounds
Black.management: The most effective method of managing the disease is through the use of resistant sugarcane varieties; if planting varieties that are susceptible to the disease then plant them in dry, well-draining soils

Black.comments: Disease transmitted through infected setts and by wind
Black.management: The disease can be successfully controlled by planting varieties of sugarcane which are resistant to the disease; disease can usually be eliminated from seed pieces by hot water treatment prior to planting; infected plants should be removed

Black.disease: Butt rot, Black rot & White leaf spot
Black.comments: Fungus survives in soil and pineapple residue; infects plants through fresh wounds
Black.management: Seed material should be stored on mother plants during dry weather and with good air circulation; freshly removed seed material should be dipped in an appropriate fungicide within 12 hours of removal from the mother plant; avoiding bruising and wounding of fruit during harvest helps to reduce black rot; harvested fruit should be dipped in an appropriate fungicide within 6-12 hours of harvest to prevent disease development during shipping

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors adverse growing conditions for avocado; more common in Guatemalan cultivars
Black.management: Avoid stressing trees by following good fertilization and irrigation practices

Black.comments: Environmental conditions may determine which fungal species is most common; spores can spread by wind or rain.
Black.management: Prune dead limbs and twigs; prune and harvest in dry conditions; provide trees with sufficient irrigation; apply a thick layer of mulch

Black.comments: Bacteria may be present on leaves but do not cause damage unless plant is stressed or bacteria enter the plant through a wound
Black.management: No treatment for disease; use certified seed; disinfected tools and cuttings; employ crop rotation; remove plant debris from soil

Black.comments: Wet soils encourage Phytophthora infection; plant in a well draining soil.
Black.management: Minimize water splash between trees by not working in a wet orchard; prune out dead limbs and twigs; remove fruit from the ground; dispose of dead wood and fruit away from trees

Black.comments: Fungus can survive in soil for up to 10 years; disease emergence is favored by soils that are held close to saturation for periods in excess of 2 weeks
Black.management: Cultural practices play an important role in the control of pink rot: the use of drip irrigation 5-8 cm below the soil surface can provide good control; deep plowing of soil and trimming back foliage to promote air circulation can also be useful; fungicides may be warranted in periods of extended cool, damp weather

Black.disease: Aphids (Carrot-williow aphids, Peach aphid, Hawthorn aohid, Melon aphid)
Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed; aphids can build up to several thousand per celery plant.
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by poor air circulation around branches and wet foliage
Black.management: Branches exhibiting dieback and severe blighting should be pruned out of the canopy; avoid overhead irrigation; if cankers appear on the trunk of the tree then it is likely it will die and it should be removed

Black.comments: Bacteria are easily spread on tools and by irrigation water; disease emergence favored by warm, moist conditions
Black.management: Chemical treatments are not available for bacterial soft rot, control relies on cultural practices; rotate crops; plant in well-draining soils or raised beds; only harvest heads when they are dry; avoid damaging heads during harvest

Black.comments: Ring spot requires cool, moist conditions to survive; disease symptoms typically develop in the fall and the peak of the infection occurs in winter
Black.management: Refrain from planting in areas known to have had disease previously; rotate crop to non-brassicas; sanitize tools and equipment regularly; apply appropriate fungicide if disease is identified in crop

Black.comments: Disease emergence in seedlings favored by cool temperatures
Black.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc.)
Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Disease has been reported worldwide
Black.management: If palm is severely diseased, it should be removed from plantation and destroyed; palms should be planted with adequate spacing to allow air to circulate between trees; remove weeds from around palms; applications appropriate broad spectrum foliar fungicides can help to protect the palms from disease

Black.comments: Beetles are nocturnal and fly at night; also a damaging pest of oil palm
Black.management: Destroy any decaying logs in plantation by chopping and burning to kill any larvae that may be inside; remove any dead trees from plantation and destroy by burning; plant a cover crop to deter egg laying by females as they do not lay eggs in areas covered by vegetation; hooked wire can be used to extract larvae that are boring into young crowns

Black.comments: Disease emergence occurs when high temperatures coincide with periods of high humidity.
Black.management: Avoid planting millet varieties that are highly susceptible to Cercospora fungi; control weeds in field; rotate crops and practice good sanitation; no chemical control is needed to treat the disease.

Black.comments: Biology and epidemiology: Individual florets are infected by air-borne spores of the causal fungus when the inflorescences are at the protogynous stage. i.e. when the stigmas have emerged before pollination has occurred. Mature spores released from the sori when the crop is threshed are mixed with seed from non-infected florets, and these are the source of infection for the subsequent crop.
Black.management: Smut is best managed by the use of resistant cultivars.

Black.comments: Crops planted early are more susceptible to the disease as they are often exposed to cooler temperatures and higher soil moisture; disease is seed transmitted and also can spread over long distances by wind.
Black.management: The most effective method to control the disease is to plant peanut varieties that have some resistance to the disease; rotation of crop with nonhost such as corn , cotton or tobacco may help to reduce inoculum in the soil; application of appropriate soil fumigants in heavily infested fields can help to control the disease.

Black.comments: Disease emergence is favored by cool, moist conditions and periods of prolonged leaf wetness.
Black.management: Remove and destroy infested crop debris to prevent build up of inoculum in soil; rotate crops away from peanut; foliar fungicide applications are largely ineffective if conditions are favorable for the disease.

Black.comments: Thrips transmit viruses to plants, including Tomato spotted wilt virus and Peanut bud necrosis virus.
Black.management: use adequate plant spacings to avoid overcrowding; provide plants with adequate irrigation and fertilization to encourage the fast establishment of seedlings and growth of a close canopy which is unattractive to thrips vectors.

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by prolonged wet weather, poorly draining soil, excessive shading of plants, overcrowded plants and lack of weed control in the plantation
Black.management: Plant vanilla using recommended spacing; control weeds around plants; remove infected parts of plants and destroy by burning; application of appropriate fungicide can help to protect the plants from disease

Black.disease: Alternaria leaf spot (Black spot, Gray spot)
Black.comments: May become a problem on cabbage during cool, wet periods.
Black.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present.

Black.comments: Disease emergence in seedlings favored by cool temperatures.
Black.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms.

Black.comments: Pathogen is spread via infected seed or by splashing water and insect movement; disease emergence favored by warm and humid conditions.
Black.management: Primary method of controlling black rot is through the use of good sanitation practices; rotate crops to non-cruciferous crops every 2 years; plant resistant varieties; control cruciferous weed species which may act as a reservoir for bacteria; plant pathogen-free seed.

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc.)
Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life.
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic.

Black.comments: Fungus overwinters in bark, dead leaves and rotting fruit; disease incidence is increased when raking is used during wet harvest of cranberries
Black.management: Disease usually manifests as a post harvest berry rot and only requires control if berries are for soft market; fungicides are available in some US states; end rot development can be slowed by refrigerating berries after harvest

Black.comments: Insect overwinters as eggs on the foliage; insect typically undergoes 2 generations per year but there may be a third generation in warmer years when there is an early spring; insect can cause substantial damage if populations are left unchecked
Black.management: Reflooding the beds for between 24 and 48 hours after eggs have hatched will kill off a high number of larvae; application of

Black.comments: Weevils overwinter as adults under the winter flooded beds; the insect undergoes only one generation per year
Black.management: Cranberry weevil populations usually remain low and little is known about their natural enemies and cultural control methods; chemicals registered for use in commercial plantations on the East coast of the US include azinphosmethyl and chlorpyrifos which should be applied if weevil population has reached the threshold (on average 4.5 weevils per 25 sweep net samples)

Black.comments: Gypsy moths are serious forest pests and can find their way into cranberry plantations by dropping from nearby trees; outbreaks are cyclic in nature and serious outbreaks in the US have tended to occur every 9-10 years; eggs can overwinter on the bed of the bogs and cause a new infestation in spring
Black.management: Larvae are easily controlled with insecticides or by reflooding the beds for a period of 24 hours

Black.disease: Alternaria leaf spot (Black spot, Gray spot)
Black.comments: May become a problem on cabbage during cool, wet periods.
Black.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present.

Black.comments: Disease emergence in seedlings favored by cool temperatures
Black.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Black.comments: Bacteria are easily spread on tools and by irrigation water; disease emergence favored by warm, moist conditions.
Black.management: Chemical treatments are not available for bacterial soft rot, control relies on cultural practices; rotate crops; plant cabbage in well-draining soils or raised beds; only harvest heads when they are dry; avoid damaging heads during harvest.

Black.comments: Ring spot requires cool, moist conditions to survive; disease symptoms typically develop in the fall and the peak of the infection occurs in winter
Black.management: Refrain from planting in areas known to have had disease previously; rotate crop to non-brassicas; sanitize tools and equipment regularly; apply appropriate fungicide if disease is identified in crop

Black.disease: Slugs & snails (Gray garden slug, Spotted garden slug, Brown garden snail, European garden snail , etc.)
Black.comments: Slugs and snails prefer moist, shaded habitats and will shelter in weeds or organic trash; adults may deposit eggs in the soil throughout the season; damage to plants can be extensive
Black.management: Practice good garden sanitation by removing garden trash, weeds and plant debris to promote good air circulation and reduce moist habitat for slugs and snails; handpick slugs at night to decrease population; spread wood ashes or eggshells around plants; attract molluscs by leaving out organic matter such as lettuce or grapefruit skins, destroy any found feeding on lure; sink shallow dishes filled with beer into the soil to attract and drown the molluscs; chemical controls include ferrous phosphate for organic gardens and metaldehyde (e.g. Buggeta)  and carbaryl (e.g Sevin bait) for non-organic growers

Black.comments: Fungi spread via wind and rain and enter plant via wounds
Black.management: Avoid wounding fruits during harvest

Black.comments: Symptoms resemble fly feeding damage; disease emerges in warm, wet conditions
Black.management: No specific treatments are currently recommended

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc)
Black.comments: Transmit several viruses including Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Damage caused by the emergence of the disease is worsened by warm, compacted soils, limited soil moisture and poor soil fertility
Black.management: There are currently no pea varieties with resistance to Fusarium root rot so control relies on cultural practices e.g. do not plant peas in same area more than once in any 5 year span or treating seeds with an appropriate fungicide prior to planting

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors high humidity and moderate temperatures; disease is common but rarely causes economic damage as it occurs mainly on old leaves and pods
Black.management: Rotating crops regularly prevents disease build-up in soil

Black.disease: Aphids (Pea aphid, Bean aphid, Cowpea aphid, Melon aphid, Peach aphid, etc.)
Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Mature larvae drop from leaves into soil to pupate; entire lifecycle can take as little as 2 weeks in warm weather; insect may go through 7 to 10 generations per year
Black.management: Check transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies

Black.comments: Beetles can decimate bean crops; beetles overwinter as adults and undergo 2-3 generations per year
Black.management: Some bean varieties may be less attractive hosts for the beetle, e.g. snapbeans are preferred hosts over lima beans; early varieties may escape damage form beetles  beetle populations can be reduced by remove overwintering sites such as brush and leaves on the ground; handpick larvae and adults; brush eggs from leaves and destroy; apply insecticidal soap to leaf undersides if infestation is heavy

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc.)
Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors warm weather; appears early in the spring
Black.management: Avoid sprinkler irrigation when fruit is ripening; rotate crops with other non-solanaceous plants

Black.comments: Disease is found wherever tomatoes are grown; ripe fruit become increasingly susceptible to the fungus the longer they stay on the vine after ripening.
Black.management: Cultural control methods such as avoiding wetting the foliage when watering and harvesting fruits as soon as they are ripe can help to reduce the incidence of the disease; in areas where disease is a persistent problem, chemical control may be necessary with an appropriate fungicide; fungicide is usually applied 4-6 weeks prior to the first anticipated fruit harvest.

Black.comments: Disease can spread rapidly after plants have set fruit; movement of air-borne spores and contact with infested soil are causes for the spread of the disease
Black.management: Apply appropriate fungicide at first sign of disease; destroy any volunteer solanaceous plants (tomato, potato, nightshade etc); practice crop rotation

Black.comments: The disease is favored by high relative humidity. Also a common disease in green house tomato crop.
Black.management: Grow available resistant varieties. Avoid leaf wetting and overhead application of water. Follow proper spacing to provide good air circulation around the plants. Remove the infected plant debris and burn them. If the disease is severe scary suitable fungicide.

Black.comments: Spread by water splash; fungus overwinters in plant debris.
Black.management: Ensure all tomato crop debris is removed and destroyed in Fall or plowed deep into soil; plant only disease-free material; avoid overhead irrigation; stake plants to increase air circulation through the foliage; apply appropriate fungicide if necessary.

Black.comments: Low calcium may result from competition from other ions in soil e.g. potassium; can also be caused by drought stress; fluctuations in soil moisture or application of excessive amounts of nitrogen fertilizer which promotes rapid vegetative growth; calcium cannot be translocated from the leaves to the fruit; foliar applications of Ca materials have not proven to reduce BER, since very little Ca is taken up by the fruit; in greenhouse production not cycling the irrigation system at night can increase BER, since night is an important period of Ca uptake.
Black.management: Maintain soil pH at 6.5; lime soil to increase the concentration of calcium in soil and decrease competition with other ions; use mulch to reduce drought stress; avoid ammonium fertilizers as they may increase competition with calcium by increasing ammonium ions in soil, use nitrate instead; avoid over fertilizing.

Black.comments: Sunscald is common on plants which have been moved suddenly from a shaded location to full sun.
Black.management: Healthy plants with full foliage are less susceptible to sunscald as the leaves provide fruits with shading, ensure plants are watered regularly and fertilized adequately; use shade cloth to protect the fruits from the sun; control diseases that develop to prevent defoliation which can lead to sunscald.

Black.comments: Can devastate tomato plantings.
Black.management: Plant resistant varieties; if signs of disease are present or if rainy conditions are likely or if using overhead irrigation appropriate fungicides should be applied.

Black.comments: Distinguishing features of aphids include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the insect's body; aphids will generally not move very quickly when disturbed; aphids may also transmit plant viruses to the plant when they feed.
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Black.comments: Adult beetles emerge in spring; female beetles lay eggs in batches of up to two dozen; eggs are orange-yellow and are laid on undersides of leaves; a female can lay 500 or more eggs over a four to five week period.
Black.management: Control of Colorado potato beetle can be challenging as they have developed high levels of insecticide resistance; adults and larvae should be hand picked from plants and destroyed in soapy water; applications of

Black.comments: Cutworms have a wide host range and attack vegetables including asparagus, bean, cabbage and other crucifers, carrot, celery, corn, lettuce, pea, pepper, potato and tomato
Black.management: Remove all plant residue from soil after harvest or at least two weeks before planting, this is especially important if the previous crop was another host such as alfalfa, beans or a leguminous cover crop; plastic or foil collars fitted around plant stems to cover the bottom 3 inches above the soil line and extending a couple of inches into the soil can prevent larvae severing plants; hand-pick larvae after dark; spread diatomaceous earth around the base of the plants (this creates a sharp barrier that will cut the insects if they try and crawl over it); apply appropriate insecticides to infested areas of garden or field if not growing organically

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc.)
Black.comments: Transmit Tomato spotted wilt virus (see disease entry); once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life.
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic.

Black.comments: Adult insect is a pale green to tan, medium sized moth; can be one of the most damaging pests of tomato; insect overwinters as pupae in the soil; the insect is also a damaging pest of corn and is also referred to as the corn earworm.
Black.management: Early treatment of tomato fruitworms is necessary as once they enter the fruit they are protected from sprays; monitor plants for eggs and young larvae and also natural enemies that could be damaged by chemicals;

Black.comments: Warm, wet weather favors disease emergence; beet very susceptible
Black.management: Treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting; plant in well draining soil; do not plant until soil is sufficiently warm

Black.comments: Beetles are generally active at night; during the day beetles hide in organic debris
Black.management: Ditches filled with water can prevent spread of beetle to/from adjacent fields; remove all weeds from garden borders; if beetle is problematic then appropriate insecticides can provide control; insecticides are usually in the form of baits

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors high humidity; fungus survives on plant debris; spores are spread by splashing water
Black.management: Avoid stressing trees by providing them with adequate irrigation and fertilization; prune out dead or dying limbs from tree canopy to reduce levels of disease inoculum; control insect pests to prevent stress to trees

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors wet weather and cool temperatures
Black.management: Fungicide application is not usually warranted unless infection occurs during wet weather which can cause severe infections

Black.comments: Disease favors warm, moist soils; spread primarily through use of infected seed pieces which may not show any outward signs of disease.
Black.management: Plant ginger in well-draining soils or on hills created by tilling; do not plant any seed pieces which show symptoms of disease; seed pieces can be treated with hot water (50°C/122°F for 10 min) or appropriate fungicides prior to planting; destroy all crop debris after harvest; keep fields weed free; do not grow ginger for more than one year in same area.

Black.comments: Fungi can survive in soil for 8-10 years
Black.management: Rotate crop to non-hosts (e.g. cereals) for at least 3 years; control weeds; avoid dense growth by planting in adequately spaced rows; disease significantly reduced by application of fungicides immediately after thinning plants; plow soil deeply

Black.comments: Bacteria are easily spread on tools and by irrigation water; disease emergence favored by warm, moist conditions; bacteria enter plant through wounds
Black.management: Chemical treatments are not available for bacterial soft rot, control relies on cultural practices; rotate crops; plant cabbage in well-draining soils or raised beds; only harvest heads when they are dry; avoid damaging heads during harvest

Black.comments: Slugs and snails prefer moist, shaded habitats and will shelter in weeds or organic trash; adults may deposit eggs in the soil throughout the season; damage to plants can be extensive
Black.management: Practice good garden sanitation by removing garden trash, weeds and plant debris to promote good air circulation and reduce moist habitat for slugs and snails; handpick slugs at night to decrease population; spread wood ashes or eggshells around plants; attract molluscs by leaving out organic matter such as lettuce or grapefruit skins, destroy any found feeding on lure; sink shallow dishes filled with beer into the soil to attract and drown the molluscs; chemical controls include ferrous phosphate for organic gardens and metaldehyde (e.g. Buggeta)  and carbaryl (e.g Sevin bait) for non-organic growers

Black.comments: Aphids will be visible on the leaves; small, rounded green to pink insects; distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Mature larvae drop from leaves into soil to pupate; entire lifecycle can take as little as 2 weeks in warm weather; insect may go through 7 to 10 generations per year
Black.management: Check  transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies

Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Occurs wherever rice is grown; fungus overwinters on plant debris; disease emergence favored by water on surfaces of plant
Black.management: Ensure plants are provided with correct nutrients and avoid water stress; chemical seed treatments are effective at reducing the incidence of the disease

Black.comments: It is seed born disease and may also affect seed germination.
Black.management: Treat seeds with hot water (52 C) for 10 min. Roughing the infected plants from field and from harvested grains. Keep the rice field and surrounding clean. Use resistant varieties. Maintain humidity in field by alternate wetting and drying.

Black.comments: Most important disease of rice worldwide; causes most damage in areas of intense cultivation; disease emergence favors high soil nitrogen content
Black.management: If disease is not endemic to the region, blast can be controlled by planting resistant rice varieties; avoid over-fertilizing crop with nitrogen as this increases the plant's susceptibility to the disease; utilize good water management to ensure plants do not suffer from drought stress; disease can be effectively controlled by the application of appropriate systemic fungicides, where available

Black.comments: Fungus survives in crop debris in soil after harvest; fruiting bodies are carried to the surface when fields are flooded where they then infect leaf sheaths at the water line
Black.management: Bury crop residue deeply in the soil after harvest; avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization; plant less susceptible rice varieties

Black.comments: Wash hands thoroughly after coming into contact with fungus
Black.management: Treat seeds with appropriate fungicide prior to planting to reduce rot in mature bulbs; avoid bruising bulbs during and after harvest; storing at temeperatures below 15°C (59°F) prevents mold from spreading but it will resume once temperature increases

Black.comments: Fungus survives in soil and disease emergence is favored by moist to wet soil
Black.management: Plant only disease-free seed; treat seed with fungicide; rotate crops with cereals or grasses to reduce levels of pathogen in soil; steam treatment or fumigation of soil can help reduce levels of Fusarium in the soil

Black.comments: Smut can persist in soil for many years and is mainly introduced through infected sets and transplants; spores can be spread by wind, on equipment or in irrigation water
Black.management: No resistance to disease known in onion; plant onions during periods which promote rapid growth; plant only health sets and transplants - if smut is present in the soil they will not become infected

Black.comments: Mature larvae drop from leaves into soil to pupate; entire lifecycle can take as little as 2 weeks in warm weather; insect may go through 7 to 10 generations per year
Black.management: Check transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by warm, rainy weather
Black.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present

Black.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Black.management: Refrain from planting in areas known to have had disease previously; rotate crop to non-brassicas; sanitize tools and equipment regularly; apply appropriate fungicide if disease is identified in crop

Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.management: Refrain from planting in areas known to have had disease previously; rotate crop to non-brassicas; sanitize tools and equipment regularly; apply appropriate fungicide if disease is identified in crop

Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus (see disease entry); once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Symptomatic plants are often found in low-lying areas of the field or garden where water accumulates; disease symptoms are similar to symptoms cuased by overwatering plants
Black.management: Plant spinach in well draining soils; carefully manage irrigation to avoid saturating soil; use seed that has beeen treatedd with fungicide; avoid planting spinach successively in the same location

Black.comments: Fungus can survive on seed and can be spread to previously uninfested fields; disease emergence is favored by warm, acidic soil
Black.management: Avoid planting spinach in soils known to be infested with Fusarium or where spinach has been planted the previous year; planting early can help protect the seedlings from the disease due to lower soil temperatures which are less favorable to the pathogen; avoid water stress to plants during flowering and seed set

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Emergence of Botrytis fruit rot is favored by cool and wet conditions; physical damage to fruit increases likelihood of infection.
Black.management: Promote air circulation around vines by using trellises or training the vines; avoid over fertilizing plants; protective fungicides can be used to control the disease and should be applied at intervals of 7-14 days from early bloom right through to harvest.

Black.comments: Female beetles deposit eggs on bark of canes and larvae burrow into primocanes.
Black.management: Canes with galls should be pruned out and burned or buried to destroy overwintering larvae; remove any wild brambles nearby which may act as a reservoir for cane borer populations; if chemical treatment is required (generally if more than 5% of canes are affected) then it should be applied after bloom to limit damage to bees.

Black.comments: Symptoms most severe on south facing side of tree exposed to winds
Black.management: In areas where disease is severe, copper fungicides should be applied in Fall and WInter prior to the first rains

Black.disease: Soft scales (Black scale, Brown soft scale , Citricolla scale)
Black.comments: Insects can produce several overlapping generations per year
Black.management: Organically acceptable methods of control include the application of horticultural oils and preservation of natural enemies

Black.comments: Disease common during wet Springs or long periods of wet weather late in season
Black.management: If disease is damaging then appropriate fungicides should be applied to whole tree

Black.comments: Serious disease of glasshouse grown citrus trees; pathogen usually drops to non-damaging levels after tree is transplanted to the field
Black.management: Keep glasshouses well lit and warm during winter to encourage vigorous root growth; use good quality potting soil which provides goo aeration

Black.comments: Symptoms most severe on south facing side of tree exposed to winds
Black.management: In areas where disease is severe, copper fungicides should be applied in Fall and WInter prior to the first rains

Black.comments: Aphids transmit tristeza virus on citrus
Black.management: Aphid numbers tend to naturally decline as leaves harden off but can be a problem on young trees or varieties which continually produce flushes of new growth; pesticides are not generally recommended due to resistance and trees can withstand a high degree of leaf curling

Black.comments: Spread by wind and rain
Black.management: Rotate crops with non-cucurbits every 1-2 years to prevent disease build-up; plant only disease free, treated seed

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc.)
Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Infections occur on new shoots after rainfall and knots develop rapidly in second year
Black.management: Prune knots on twigs and branches 8-10 cm (3-4 in) below the swelling and remove the pruned branches from the orchard; remove knots on older branches by removing the knot plus 2 cm (0.8 in) of surrounding tissue; removal of knots is most effective when done in midsummer; disease can be controlled by application of appropriate fungicides, if available, during shoot elongation

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors warm temperatures.
Black.management: If disease is damaging then appropriate fungicides should be applied to whole tree.

Black.comments: Serious disease of glasshouse grown citrus trees; pathogen usually drops to non-damaging levels after tree is transplanted to the field.
Black.management: Keep glasshouses well lit and warm during winter to encourage vigorous root growth; use good quality potting soil which provides goo aeration.

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors wet, cool and windy weather.
Black.management: In areas where disease is severe, copper fungicides should be applied in Fall and WInter prior to the first rains.

Black.disease: Aphids (Black citrus aphid and Brown citrus aphid)
Black.comments: Aphids transmit tristeza virus on citrus.
Black.management: Aphid numbers tend to naturally decline as leaves harden off but can be a problem on young trees or varieties which continually produce flushes of new growth; pesticides are not generally recommended due to resistance and trees can withstand a high degree of leaf curling.

Black.disease: Soft scales (Black scale, Brown soft scale , Citricolla scale)
Black.comments: Insects can produce several overlapping generations per year.
Black.management: Organically acceptable methods of control include the application of horticultural oils and preservation of natural enemies.

Black.comments: Palms may recover from disease; fungi can enter through pruning wounds
Black.management: Prune out infected fronds, leaf bases and inflorescenses and burn the material immediately; pruning wounds should be protected by spraying with Bordeaux mixture

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by high humidity
Black.management: Infected leaves should be pruned out and destroyed; disease can be controlled by sprays of Bordeaux mixture or other appropriate broad spectrum fungicide

Black.comments: Beetles are nocturnal and fly at night; also a damaging pest of oil palm
Black.management: Destroy any decaying logs in plantation by chopping and burning to kill any larvae that may be inside; remove any dead trees from plantation and destroy by burning; plant a cover crop to deter egg laying by females as they do not lay eggs in areas covered by vegetation; hooked wire can be used to extract larvae that are boring into young crowns

Black.comments: Disease can be spread long distance by the movement of infected seedlings or within the field by water splash; bacteria can enter the plant through wounds
Black.management: Protective sprays of copper should be applied to the plants just before the onset of the rainy season and should be continued right through to the short rains

Black.disease: Cercospora leaf spot (Brown eye spot, Berry blotch)
Black.comments: Disease can be spread by wind, water-splash and through human movement through fields, particularly when plants are wet
Black.management: Ensure crop is adequately fertilized as nutrient deficient plants are more susceptible to the disease; remove all crop debris from filed after pruning to prevent build up of inoculum; good plant spacing and pruning to open up the canopy promotes good air circulation around foliage and protects against disease; if disease does occur then it can be controlled with the use of copper fungicides where available

Black.comments: Damage caused by the beetles promotes secondary infestation by bacteria and other fungi; adult beetles overwinter in the plant
Black.management: Prune out infested twigs and stems and destroy; flagging branches should be pruned back a few inches from the beginning of symptomatic areas; adequate fertilizer and irrigation to ensure vigorous plants can speed recovery from pruning injury

Black.comments: Female beetle lays clusters of eggs inside the berries; insect undergoes up to 5 generations per year
Black.management: Removal of dropped berries and debris on plantation floor can help reduce sources of new infections; remove any berries remaining on plants after harvest; insecticide application is only effective if applied when the female beetle is still in the entry tunnel and has not yet penetrated deep into the berry

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors warm, wet weather
Black.management: Plant only certified, disease-free seed; treat seeds with hot water prior to planting; rotate crops to non-brassica species; irrigate plants in morning to allow sufficient time to dry out during the day; apply appropriate fungicide

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors cool, wet soils
Black.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Black.comments: Fungus can survive in soil for prolonged periods
Black.management: Plant resistant radish varieties; rotate crops with non-brassica species

Black.comments: Caused by low calcium concentration in fruit; may result from competition from other competitive ions in soil e.g. potassium, drought stress; fluctuations in soil moisture or application of excessive amounts of nitrogen fertilizer which promotes rapid vegetative growth.
Black.management: Maintain soil pH at 6.5; lime soil to increase the concentration of calcium in soil and decrease competition with other ions; use mulch to reduce drought stress; avoid ammonium fertilizers as they may increase competition with calcium by increasing ammonium ions in soil, use nitrate instead; avoid over fertilizing.

Black.comments: Disease can spread rapidly after plants have set fruit
Black.management: Apply fungicide at first sign of disease; destroy any volunteer solanaceous plants (tomato, potato, nightshade etc); practice crop rotation

Black.comments: Distinguishing features of aphids include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the insect's body; aphids will generally not move very quickly when disturbed; aphids may also transmit plant viruses to the plant when they feed.
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Black.comments: Adult beetles emerge in spring; female beetles lay eggs in batches of up to two dozen; eggs are orange-yellow and are laid on undersides of leaves; a female can lay 500 or more eggs over a four to five week period.
Black.management: Control of Colorado potato beetle can be challenging as they have developed high levels of insecticide resistance; in the home garden planting early maturing varieties of potato allows the plants to escape from most damage; adults and larvae should be hand picked from plants and destroyed in soapy water; applications of

Black.comments: Cutworms have a wide host range and attack vegetables including asparagus, bean, cabbage and other crucifers, carrot, celery, corn, lettuce, pea, pepper, potato and tomato
Black.management: Remove all plant residue from soil after harvest or at least two weeks before planting, this is especially important if the previous crop was another host such as alfalfa, beans or a leguminous cover crop; plastic or foil collars fitted around plant stems to cover the bottom 3 inches above the soil line and extending a couple of inches into the soil can prevent larvae severing plants; hand-pick larvae after dark; spread diatomaceous earth around the base of the plants (this creates a sharp barrier that will cut the insects if they try and crawl over it); apply appropriate insecticides to infested areas of garden or field if not growing organically

Black.comments: Fungus survives in seeds or sunflower debris in the field; disease emergence favors periods of wet weather during flowering
Black.management: Rotate crop to a non-host (e.g. small grains) a period of 4 years; plant hybrids which are more tolerant of the disease; control stem weevil populations in sunflower fields

Black.comments: Conditions which favor the development of the disease mach those that are favorable for the host plant; disease emergence is favored by periods of high humidity where leaves remain dry
Black.management: Allow adequate spacing between plants to promote good air circulation around foliage; plant sunflowers in an area that receives full sun for most of the day; remove and destroy all sunflower crop debris after harvest; applications of appropriate foliar fungicides can help control the disease but care should be taken as some labels do not allow seeds from treated plants to be used as food or feed

Black.comments: Little is known about the survival and spread of the pathogen which causes the disease; spores are believed to be spread by splashing water; disease will develop rapidly during periods of moderate to warm weather with high rainfall
Black.management: Plant high quality seed which is free of diseases; rotate crop away from sunflower for a period of 3 years, especially if overhead irrigation is used; fungicides are rarely required for the treatment of Septoria leaf spot

Black.comments: Fungus is soil-borne and enters plants through the roots, invading the vascular system; pathogen can be spread to uninfested fields through contaminated irrigation water or movement of infested soils
Black.management: Plant high quality, disease-free seed; avoid planting sunflowers in fields known to have been infested with Verticillium previously; plant resistant sunflower hybrids in areas where disease is known to be problematic

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by moderate temperature, poor air circulation around plant and shady conditions
Black.management: Disease does not cause severe damage to hazelnut and control is not warranted

Black.comments: Common in cool moist regions on a variety of crops; fungus can survive in soil for 8-10 years
Black.management: Rotate crop to non-hosts (e.g. cereals) for at least 3 years; control weeds; avoid dense growth by planting in adequately spaced rows; disease significantly reduced by application of fungicides immediately after thinning plants; plow soil deeply

Black.comments: Bacteria are easily spread on tools and by irrigation water; disease emergence favored by warm, moist conditions; bacteria enter plant through wounds
Black.management: Chemical treatments are not available for bacterial soft rot, control relies on cultural practices; rotate crops; plant cabbage in well-draining soils or raised beds; only harvest heads when they are dry; avoid damaging heads during harvest

Black.disease: Slugs & snails (Gray garden slug, Spotted garden slug, Brown garden snail, European garden snail , etc.)
Black.comments: Slugs and snails prefer moist, shaded habitats and will shelter in weeds or organic trash; adults may deposit eggs in the soil throughout the season; damage to plants can be extensive
Black.management: Practice good garden sanitation by removing garden trash, weeds and plant debris to promote good air circulation and reduce moist habitat for slugs and snails; handpick slugs at night to decrease population; spread wood ashes or eggshells around plants; attract molluscs by leaving out organic matter such as lettuce or grapefruit skins, destroy any found feeding on lure; sink shallow dishes filled with beer into the soil to attract and drown the molluscs; chemical controls include ferrous phosphate for organic gardens and metaldehyde (e.g. Buggeta)  and carbaryl (e.g Sevin bait) for non-organic growers

Black.disease: Aphids (Green peach aphid, Lettuce aphid, Plum aphid)
Black.comments: Aphids will be visible on the leaves; small, rounded green to pink insects; distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Beetles are generally active at night; during the day beetles hide in organic debris
Black.management: Ditches filled with water can prevent spread of beetle to/from adjacent fields; remove all weeds from garden borders; if beetle is problematic then appropriate insecticides can provide control; insecticides are usually in the form of baits

Black.comments: Mature larvae drop from leaves into soil to pupate; entire lifecycle can take as little as 2 weeks in warm weather; insect may go through 7 to 10 generations per year
Black.management: Check  transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies

Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Fungus survives in buds and twigs; spores spread by water splash.
Black.management: Application of Bordeaux mixture before rains in Fall are sufficient to protect dormant buds and twigs over winter.

Black.comments: Infestations usually appear in small pockets in orchards; insect eggs overwinter and hatch in Spring.
Black.management: Organically grown trees can be sprayed with neem oil to control aphid populations; chemical control of the aphid is rarely necessary.

Black.comments: Peach twig borers overwinter as larvae in a specialized cell known as a hibernaculum; overwintering sites are located in rough areas of bark on 1 to 4 year old wood in crotch of limbs.
Black.management: Most effective method of treatment is well-timed applications of insecticide around time of bloom; organically acceptable insecticides include

Black.comments: Anthracnose is found in all major vegetable growing areas worldwide and can cause large losses if left untreated
Black.management: Always plant disease-free seeds and transplants; seeds can be freed from infection by treating with hot water at 52°C (125.6°F) for 30 minutes; if disease is known to present, the field should be rotated with non-susceptible crops for a period of 3 years; plowing crop residue deeply into the soil r removing crop debris can help to reduce inoculum in the soil

Black.comments: Wet conditions promote growth and spread of disease; spread by rainfall through plant or banana bunch
Black.management: Commercially produced fruit should be washed and dipped in fungicide prior to shipping; protect fruit from injury; remove flower parts which can harbour fungus

Black.comments: Colonies are often tended by ants; populations can build rapidly during warm weather
Black.management: Chemical control does not provide protection against transmission of Banana bunchy top and direct feeding damage is not usually severe enough to warrant spraying; insecticidal soaps can help control aphid populations; plants infected with bunch top should be removed and destroyed to prevent spread

Black.comments: Insects are nocturnal, feeding and mating only at night;
Black.management: Plant only healthy plant material, do not plant if any tunnels are visible; hot water treatment of clean trimmed suckers can be used to kill off many eggs and grubs; applications of neem powder can reduce weevil numbers; appropriate insecticides applied at time of planting can help control weevil numbers

Black.comments: Disease highly water dependent and spores can be spread by water splash; disease can move root to root to infect a row of plants
Black.management: Plant disease free transplants; manage water carefully as pathogen depends on water for survival; rotate crops; apply appropriate fungicide

Black.management: Check  transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies

Black.comments: Weevil will go through multiple generations per year; pepper preferred host but weevil will feed on other plants in the nightshade family also
Black.management: Remove any nightshade plants growing in close proximity to pepper; immediately remove any dropped fruit from soil surface; if pepper weevil becomes problematic, rotate crop the following year; organically acceptable control methods include sprays of pyrethrin; destroy pepper crop residue immediately after harvest

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc.)
Black.comments: Insect transmits Tomato spotted wilt virus (see disease entry); once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Adult insect is a pale green to tan, medium sized moth; can be one of the most damaging pests of tomato; insect overwinters as pupae in the soil; the insect is also a damaging pest of corn and is also referred to as the corn earworm
Black.management: Early treatment of tomato fruitworms is necessary as once they enter the fruit they are protected from sprays; monitor plants for eggs and young larvae and also natural enemies that could be damaged by chemicals;

Black.management: Plant disease free transplants; manage water carefully as pathogen depends on water for survival; rotate crops; apply appropriate fungicide.
