Black.comments: Avoid overhead irrigation and excessive application of nitrogen.
Black.management: Plants that are stressed are more susceptible to the disease, ensure plants are provided with water and fertilizer; delaying harvest until diseased fruit has dropped from tree can reduce the number of fruit lost to the disease post harvest; preharvest treatment with fungicide is often ineffective.

Black.comments: Widespread in areas of the Southern hemisphere with summer rainfall; fungus survives in decomposing leaves on/in soil around infected trees; spores can be spread by wind and water splash.
Black.management: Remove leaf litter from around trees to reduce inoculum; keep trees well irrigated during dry periods to reduce leaf fall; apply copper containing fungicides where appropriate.

Black.comments: Serious disease in humid tropical and subtropical areas; disease spreads rapidly over short distances.
Black.management: Control of the disease is reliant on timely sprays of copper containing fungicides and the provision of windbreaks to reduce spread of inoculum from infected trees.

Black.comments: Disease overwinters in plant debris; occurs worldwide
Black.management: The most effective method of controlling the disease is to plant yam varieties that are resistant to anthracnose such as TDA 291 or TDA 297

Black.disease: Dry rot disease (caused by yam nematode)
Black.comments: Some time the infected tubers may not show external cracking which make it difficult to diagnose. In that case scrap out the external layer of tuber to check the disease incidence.
Black.management: Use disease free tubers/setts for planting. Treating tubers with hot water for 40 min at 50-55 C before sowing and after harvest to reduce disease both in field and storage. In Africa smearing tubers with wood ash or cow dung shows reduced nematode infection in field. Follow crop rotation with non host or antagonist crops like ground nut, sorghum, maize, chill pepper etc.

Black.comments: More common during periods of high rainfall
Black.management: Harvest infected alfalfa early to avoid severe infections which reduce hay quality; rotating crops may reduce incidence of disease

Black.comments: Disease more prevalent in water saturated soil.
Black.management: Grow resistant cultivars; try to improve drainage if soil has tendency to be waterlogged.

Black.disease: Aphids (Cowpea aphid, Blue alfalfa aphid, Pea aphid, etc)
Black.comments: Blue alfalfa aphid and pea aphids prefer cooler temperatures and are most abundant in Spring and Fall; cowpea aphids are generally a sporadic pest but are most common in Spring.
Black.management: Insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Black.comments: Mature larvae drop from leaves into soil to pupate; entire lifecycle can take as little as 2 weeks in warm weather; insect may go through 7 to 10 generations per year
Black.management: Check  transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies

Black.disease: Alternaria leaf spot (Black spot, Gray spot)
Black.comments: May become a problem during cool, wet periods
Black.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present

Black.comments: Disease emergence in seedlings favored by cool temperatures
Black.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc.)
Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Disease emergence is favored by wet conditions; fungus is spread by splashing water; faba beans and filed peas are alternate hosts for the fungus
Black.management: There are no varieties of lentil which are completely resistant to the disease but some are less susceptible than others and can be planted in areas where disease is prevalent; a well-timed application of an appropriate fungicide may be enough to control the disease but should be reapplied in the event of persistent wet weather

Black.comments: Disease can be transmitted to seedlings from infected seeds; foliar transmission is favored by cool, moist soils
Black.management: Plant only disease-free seed or seed treated with a fungicide prior to planting; rotate crop away from lentil for a period of three years; remove and destroy all lentil crop residue after harvest to reduce the levels of inoculum the following growing season; a single application of an appropriate foliar fungicide is usually enough to control the disease but should be reapplied during persistent wet weather

Black.comments: Fungus had a wide host range and affects beans, tobacco, soybean, pigeon pea and many other crops; disease is primarily spread via microsclerota in the soil
Black.management: Organic soil amendments such as the addition of manure or neemcake can be used to reduce levels of inocuum in the soil

Black.comments: Insect is a damaging pest of black pepper in Malaysia and Indonesia
Black.management: Small populations of the insect can be hand picked from plants and destroyed; insecticide application may be required to control high populations

Black.comments: Fungus had a wide host range and affects beans, tobacco, soybean, pigeon pea and many other crops; disease is primarily spread via microsclerota in the soil
Black.management: Organic soil amendments such as the addition of manure or neemcake can be used to reduce levels of inocuum in the soil

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Some dwarf types of cashew are resistant to this disease; disease is widespread in cashew growing regions of Brazil
Black.management: Currently no control measures are used

Black.comments: Adults large and gray-brown with knobbly appearance; larvae legless grubs which are white with a brown head
Black.management: Remove bark from infested areas and destroy any larvae or pupae found, this process should be repeated every month for up to six months; severely infested trees should be removed and destroyed; remove all adult weevils from tree prior to destruction and also remove bark and kill all larvae and pupae

Black.comments: Disease emergence in seedlings favored by cool temperatures
Black.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Black.comments: Disease favors cool, humid weather
Black.management: Remove all crop debris after harvest; rotate with non-brassicas; application of appropriate fungicides may be required if symptoms of disease are present

Black.comments: Pathogen is spread via infected seed or by splashing water and insect movement; disease emergence favored by warm and humid conditions
Black.management: Primary method of controlling black rot is through the use of good sanitation practices; rotate crops to non-cruciferous crops every 2 years; plant resistant varieties; control cruciferous weed species which may act as a reservoir for bacteria; plant pathogen-free seed

Black.comments: Serious disease of glasshouse grown citrus trees; pathogen usually drops to non-damaging levels after tree is transplanted to the field.
Black.management: Keep glasshouses well lit and warm during winter to encourage vigorous root growth; use good quality potting soil which provides good aeration.

Black.comments: Symptoms most severe on south facing side of tree exposed to winds.
Black.management: In areas where disease is severe, copper fungicides should be applied in Fall and WInter prior to the first rains.

Black.comments: Aphids transmit tristeza virus on citrus.
Black.management: Aphid numbers tend to naturally decline as leaves harden off but can be a problem on young trees or varieties which continually produce flushes of new growth; pesticides are not generally recommended due to resistance and trees can withstand a high degree of leaf curling.

Black.comments: Insects can produce several overlapping generations per year.

Black.disease: Cucumber beetles (Western striped cucumber beetle, Western spotted cucumber beetle, Banded cucumber beetle)
Black.comments: Beetles overwinter in soil and leaf litter and emerge from soil when temperatures begin to reach and exceed 12.7°C (55°F).
Black.management: Monitor new planting regularly for signs of beetle; floating row covers can be used to protect the plants from damage but will need to be removed at bloom to allow bees to pollinate plants; applications of kaolin clay can be effective for management of small beetle populations; application of appropriate insecticides may be necessary.

Black.comments: Mature larvae drop from leaves into soil to pupate; entire lifecycle can take as little as 2 weeks in warm weather; insect may go through 7 to 10 generations per year
Black.management: Check transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies

Black.comments: Squash bugs overwinter in crop debris or under rocks and stones
Black.management: Destroy all crops residue as soon as possible after harvest or on plant death; apply row covers at planting; apply insecticidal soap or appropriate insecticide

Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Yellowing leaves may be mistaken for nutrient deficiency; can be spread by contaminated seed.
Black.management: Grow tolerant varieties; apply protective fungicide; ensure good air circulation around greenhouse grown plants; use drip irrigation to avoid wetting foliage.

Black.comments: High humidity and overhead watering promotes spread of disease.
Black.management: No treatment when present; use disease free seed and/or transplants; use wide field spacing to promote air circulation around plants; remove diseased leaves from plant and soil surface immediately.

Black.disease: Slugs & snails (Gray garden slug, Spotted garden slug, Brown garden snail, European garden snail , etc.)
Black.comments: Slugs and snails prefer moist, shaded habitats and will shelter in weeds or organic trash; adults may deposit eggs in the soil throughout the season; damage to plants can be extensive.
Black.management: Practice good garden sanitation by removing garden trash, weeds and plant debris to promote good air circulation and reduce moist habitat for slugs and snails; handpick slugs at night to decrease population; spread wood ashes or eggshells around plants; attract molluscs by leaving out organic matter such as lettuce or grapefruit skins, destroy any found feeding on lure; sink shallow dishes filled with beer into the soil to attract and drown the molluscs; chemical controls include ferrous phosphate for organic gardens and metaldehyde (e.g. Buggeta) and carbaryl (e.g Sevin bait) for non-organic growers.

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed.
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Black.comments: Mature larvae drop from leaves into soil to pupate; entire lifecycle can take as little as 2 weeks in warm weather; insect may go through 7 to 10 generations per year.
Black.management: Check transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies.

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors wet soils
Black.management: Avoid planting seeds too deeply; do not plant seeds too thickly to promote air circulation around seedlings; do not over-water plants

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors cold, wet weather
Black.management: In areas where disease is severe, copper fungicides should be applied in Fall and WInter

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors warm rainy days during bloom; care should be taken to time antibiotic application properly
Black.management: Cut out diseased wood; treat with Bordeaux mixture or approved fixed copper materials for organic production; streptomycin or copper application to blossoms may be necessary to prevent spread

Black.comments: Disease transmitted through infected seed; fungus can survive in crop debris in soil and reinfect crop the following season
Black.management: Plant resistant varieties; use certified disease free seed; avoid sprinkler irrigation, water plants at base; plow bean crop debris into soil

Black.comments: Fungus can survive in soil for several years
Black.management: Practice long term crop rotation; avoid over or under watering plants

Black.management: Monitor plants for eggs and young larvae and also natural enemies that could be damaged by chemicals;

Black.management: Check  transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies

Black.comments: Bacteria are carried on tubers and in wounds and can be spread to healthy tubers during handling and cutting of seed pieces; disease emergence favors high soil temperatures
Black.management: Plant seed pieces which are the product of tissue culture; sanitize tools and equipment when cutting seed pieced to prevent bacterial contamination; avoid damaging tubers during harvest; reduce periods of leaf wetness by allowing enough time for leaves to dry throughout the day after watering

Black.comments: Disease is most severe during warm and dry conditions
Black.management: Common scab can be very difficult to manage and prevention of the disease relies on combining several different methods. These include: avoiding planting infected tubers, using a 3-4 rotation away from potato; planting less susceptible potato varieties (none are immune); maintaining a high soil moisture content for 4-6 weeks after stolon tips begin to swell at the onset of tuber development; amending soil to lower pH and treating seed with appropriate fungicides when available

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors poorly draining soil; poor aeration of soil and high temperatures; disease symptoms are most severe in coarse soils that are low in nitrogen
Black.management: Stressed plants are more susceptible to black dot; rotate crops away from potato; do not plant infested tubers or seed pieces; fertilize and water plants adequately; protective fungicides can be applied where available

Black.comments: Fungus can be spread by infested soil or planting infected seed pieces and tubers; disease emergence favors cool, moist soil
Black.management: No potato varieties are completely resistant to the disease; control relies on reducing the level of inoculum in both the soil and in tubers; methods include applying fungicide to seed pieces or soil; avoiding planting seeds too deeply in cold soils and rotating crop away from potato to reduce levels of soil inoculum

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors high soil water saturation late in the season
Black.management: Plant potato in well-draining soils with no history of pink rot; avoid overwatering plants; avoid wounding during harvest

Black.comments: Aphids are most damaging to potato through the transmission of viruses such as Potato leafroll virus; distinguishing aphid features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.comments: Adult beetles emerge in spring; female beetles lay eggs in batches of up to two dozen; eggs are orange-yellow and are laid on undersides of leaves; a female can lay 500 or more eggs over a four to five week period
Black.management: Control of Colorado potato beetle can be challenging as they have developed high levels of insecticide resistance; in the home garden planting early maturing varieties of potato allows the plants to escape from most damage; adults and larvae should be hand picked from plants and destroyed in soapy water; applications of

Black.comments: Fungi survive on dried leaves; disease emergence is favored by heavy shading of plants
Black.management: If infection is severe it can be treated with 1% Bordeaux mixture; avoid shading plants and follow good sanitary practices to limit spread

Black.disease: Alternaria leaf spot (Black spot, Gray spot)
Black.comments: May become a problem during cool, wet periods
Black.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present

Black.comments: Fungus can survive in soil for prolonged periods
Black.management: Control depends on crop rotation with non-brassica species

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors cool, wet soils
Black.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Black.comments: Transmitted by many species of aphid, including cabbage aphids and peach aphids
Black.management: Use of reflective mulches may help to deter aphid feeding; applications of appropriate insecticides may reduce secondary spread of virus

Black.comments: Disease transmitted through infected seed; fungus can survive in crop debris in soil and reinfect crop the following season.
Black.management: Plant resistant varieties; use certified disease free seed; avoid sprinkler irrigation, water plants at base; plow bean crop debris into soil.

Black.comments: Adults large butterflies with black-brown wings and white markings; larvae pale yellow caterpillars with black markings
Black.management: Larvae and pupae should be handpicked from trees where possible; application of appropriate foliar insecticide may be required to control heavy infestations

Black.disease: Alternaria leaf spot (Black spot, Gray spot)
Black.comments: May become a problem on cabbage during cool, wet periods
Black.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by wet foliage and warm weather; rain and fog enhance the development of the disease; fungus survives in soil on crop debris but is killed when the debris decomposes
Black.management: Disease can be difficult to control in wet, warm conditions; apply appropriate fungicides when first symptoms appear or as a protective measure in humid areas; treat seeds with fungicide or hot water prior to planting; apply gibberellic acid to carrot foliage to promote upright growth and promote air circulation through canopy

Black.comments: Disease is spread through infected seed and can survive in soil for up to 8 years
Black.management: Black rot is difficult to control and can survive in the soil for long periods of time: practice long crop rotations ; plow crop residue into soil immediately after harvest; plant resistant varieties; plant only pathogen-free seed; treat seeds with hot water prior to planting

Black.comments: Fungi enters fruit through wounds.
Black.management: Appropriate protective fungicides should be applied; dipping fruits in hot water at 48°C for 20 minutes reduces the incidence of the disease.

Black.comments: Disease usually enters orchard from infected papaya leaves in adjacent orchards.
Black.management: Applications of appropriate protective fungicides at intervals of 14 to 28 days provide satisfactory control of the disease.

Black.comments: Scale insects overwinter in the black cap stage; winged adult males mate with females which retain their eggs inside the body until they hatch.
Black.management: Populations are often kept in check by natural enemies, including predacious beetles and some wasps - although broad-spectrum insecticides may result in outbreaks of scale by killing off populations of beneficial insects; trees can be sprayed with horticultural oils when dormant which effectively kill scales without damaging natural enemies.

Black.comments: The disease is favored by wet weather conditions. The pathogen is more prevalent in Taiwan, Nigeria, Pakistan, Central America and India.
Black.management: Remove and burn the infected leaves. Follow crop rotation. Keep the field free from weeds. Follow proper crop density. If the disease is severe apply suitable fungicides.

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors poorly drained soil and warm weather.
Black.management: Rotating the crop away from tobacco for at least one year will help to reduce levels of inoculum; plant tobacco varieties that have some degree of resistance to the disease; apply appropriate fungicides to the soil; plant tobacco in well draining soils; destroy stalks and roots immediately after harvest to reduce overwintering sites for the pathogen; control nematodes in the soil.

Black.comments: Serious disease of glasshouse grown tobacco plants.
Black.management: Reduce build-up of moisture in glasshouses by increasing ventilation and air circulation; increase frequency of leaf clipping and reduce the amount of leaves removed at each clipping; avoid injury to seedlings.

Black.comments: The disease is most damaging in fields where tobacco has been grown the previous year and in wet areas of fields; bacterium can also colonize other Solanaceous crops such as tomato, pepper and eggplant as well as several weed species.
Black.management: One of the most important management strategies for Granville wilt is crop rotation as the bacteria that cause the disease do not survive well in the absence of the tobacco host plant; a rotation away from tobacco for as little as one year is highly beneficial; some tobacco varieties are more susceptible to the disease than others, although none are completely immune, and should be grown when the disease is of concern; all tobacco crop debris should be removed and destroyed following harvest to reduce inoculum levels.

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed.
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Black.comments: Fungus survive in plant debris in soil.
Black.management: Rotate crops with non-susceptible grasses; avoid excess irrigation or drought stress.

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed.

Black.comments: Adult insect is a pale green to tan, medium sized moth; insect is also very damaging pests of corn; insect overwinters as pupae in the soil.

Black.management: Check transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies.

Black.comments: Beetles can decimate bean crops; beetles overwinter as adults and undergo 2-3 generations per year
Black.management: Some bean varieties may be less attractive hosts for the beetle, e.g. snapbeans are preferred hosts over lima beans; early varieties may escape damage form beetles  beetle populations can be reduced by remove overwintering sites such as brush and leaves on the ground; handpick larvae and adults; brush eggs from leaves and destroy; apply insecticidal soap to leaf undersides if infestation is heavy

Black.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed.

Black.disease: Cucumber beetles (Western striped cucumber beetle, Western spotted cucumber beetle, Banded cucumber beetle)
Black.comments: Beetles overwinter in soil and leaf litter and emerge from soil when temperatures begin to reach and exceed 12.7°C (55°F).
Black.management: Monitor new planting regularly for signs of beetle; floating row covers can be used to protect the plants from damage but will need to be removed at bloom to allow bees to pollinate plants; applications of kaolin clay can be effective for management of small beetle populations; application of appropriate insecticides may be necessary.

Black.disease: Thrips (Western flower thrips, Onion thrips, etc.)
Black.comments: Transmit viruses such as Tomato spotted wilt virus; once acquired, the insect retains the ability to transmit the virus for the remainder of its life.
Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic.

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors warm, humid conditions
Black.management: Plant only disease free material; if disease is known to be present rotate crops with non-hosts such as cereals which are a non-host

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors high temperature and high humidity; fungus survives on plant debris
Black.management: The disease is difficult to control and all pistachio cultivars used commercially are susceptible; applications of appropriate fungicides may help to reduce the incidence of the disease; good irrigation practices should be followed; practices that reduce the wetness of the ground around plants is recommended

Black.comments: Fungus survives in dead infected material that remains on trees
Black.management: The disease is very difficult to control and treatment relies on a combination of pruning, fungicide application and good irrigation practices; multiple applications of an appropriate fungicide are required over the summer months; infected areas should be pruned by cutting 5 cm below symptomatic areas to reduce inoculum the following year; if sprinkler irrigation is used then water should be directed away from the tree canopy

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by moderate temperature, poor air circulation around plant and shady conditions
Black.management: Disease does not cause severe damage to pistachio and control is not warranted

Black.comments: Fungi overwinter in fallen leaves from previous season; disease is common in Mediterranean growing regions and in the Middle East
Black.management: Control of the disease relies on preventative applications of appropriate fungicides which should be started when the first leaves expand and be repeated monthly thereafter

Black.comments: Serious disease of pistachio in Australia; pathogen enters plant through wounds; can be spread by contaminated pruning shears
Black.management: Disinfect tools and equipment thoroughly between use

Black.comments: Disease emergence is favored by rainfall and water saturated soil.
Black.management: Diseased fruits should be removed and destroyed; infected twigs and branches should be pruned out; applications of appropriate fungicides can help to control the disease.

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors very wet weather conditions; disease is usually introduced to non-infected areas by infected seed
Black.management: Plant only certified seed; if disease is known to present rotate crops with non-hosts such as cereals; if problem is severe then a 4 year rotation away from susceptible plants may be required; there are no seed treatments or fungicides available to treat the disease

Black.comments: Black aphids can cause severe damage when infestation is high but more commonly cause problems in chickpea crops through the transmission of several plant viruses
Black.management: Plants should be monitored for signs of aphids; if insects are present then control measures should be implemented; some chickpea cultivars are more susceptible to aphid infestation than others - cultivars with a low density of trichomes (leaf hairs) are more susceptible than others; chemical control may be warranted although aphids have developed resistance to several insecticides in India; cultural control methods include early planting which promotes early closure of the plant canopy and reduced the spread of viruses; aphid infestations tend to be worse when broad plant spacing is employed

Black.comments: The bacteria spreads mainly by splashing rainfall, mechanical and insect injuries. The pathogen also infects other crops like bean, eggplant, lettuce, and pepper.
Black.management: Use healthy and disease free seeds.

Black.comments: Warm, wet weather favors disease emergence; beet very susceptible.
Black.management: Treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting; plant in well draining soil; do not plant until soil is sufficiently warm.

Black.comments: The pathogen survive on weeds like pigweed, Kochia, and lambs quarters during off season.
Black.management: Plant resistant varieties. Crop rotation with non host crop. Keep field free from weeds.

Black.comments: Beetles are generally active at night; during the day beetles hide in organic debris.
Black.management: Ditches filled with water can prevent spread of beetle to/from adjacent fields; remove all weeds from garden borders; if beetle is problematic then appropriate insecticides can provide control; insecticides are usually in the form of baits.

Black.comments: Mature larvae drop from leaves into soil to pupate; entire lifecycle can take as little as 2 weeks in warm weather; insect may go through 7 to 10 generations per year.
Black.management: Check transplants for signs of leafminer damage prior to planting; remove plants from soil immediately after harvest; only use insecticides when leafminer damage has been identified as unnecessary spraying will also reduce populations of their natural enemies.

Black.comments: Plants that are planted in infected soil become infected by splashing water and soil; fungus survives in soil for up to 9 months.
Black.management: Fumigating soil may help reduce soil inoculum; solarizing soil may destroy soil inoculum; rotate to non-host crops if funigation or solarization is not possible; wash all soil from plant crowns prior to planting; weed around plants regularly; plant only disease free transplants; do not use excessive amounts of nitrogen fertilizer;

Black.comments: Slugs prefer moist, shaded habitats and will shelter in weeds or organic trash; adults may deposit eggs in the soil throughout the season; damage to plants can be extensive.
Black.management: Practice good garden sanitation by removing garden trash, weeds and plant debris to promote good air circulation and reduce moist habitat for slugs; handpick slugs at night to decrease population; spread wood ashes or eggshells around plants; attract molluscs by leaving out organic matter such as lettuce or grapefruit skins, destroy any found feeding on lure; sink shallow dishes filled with beer into the soil to attract and drown the molluscs; chemical controls include ferrous phosphate for organic gardens and metaldehyde (e.g. Buggeta) and carbaryl (e.g Sevin bait) for non-organic growers.

Black.disease: Aphids (Peach aphid, Potato aphid, Melon aphid, Strawberry aphid)
Black.comments: Aphids transmit several viruses which can be very damaging to strawberry, the insects rarely reach a high enough population to cause severe damage directly but the spread of viruses is a major concern in strawberry production.
Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Black.comments: Fungus is transferred to fruit by some species of fly, thrips and beetle; fig varieties with smaller ostioles (pores) on the fruit are less susceptible to the disease than those with large ostioles.
Black.management: Remove all old fruit an crop debris from orchard; try to reduce dust around trees.

Black.comments: Spores rupture out from protective membrane on heads; fungus can survive in infected seed
Black.management: Use only certified smut-free seed; treat seeds with hot water prior to planting to kill fungi; treat seeds with systemic fungicide (fungi inside seed) fungicide; grow resistant varieties

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors heavy nitrogen fertilization; high humidity and cool temperatures
Black.management: Planting resistant varieties is one of the best ways to protect plants from powdery mildew; other control strategies include: application of appropriate foliar fungicides, if available; removal of crop debris from field after harvest to reduce the level of overwintering fungus; removal of volunteer wheat plants which can act as a reservoir for the disease

Black.disease: Aphids (Bird cherry-oat aphid, Russian wheat aphid, Corn leaf aphid, etc.)
Black.comments: Fields should be checked for aphid populations periodically after emergence
Black.management: Sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use; in commercial plantations aphid numbers are usually kept in check by predators and natural enemies; beneficial insect populations should be assessed before chemical control is considered; if no beneficial insect populations are present and aphids are damaging then apply appropriate insecticides

Black.comments: Fungus can survive in soil for up to 5 years; severely infected pastures can be reduced to masses of rotting plants on the ground
Black.management: Allowing close grazing or cutting of the stand reduces humidity around plants which in turn will slow the progression of the disease; plow plant material deeply into soil; rotation to a non-susceptible host for a period of 4-5 years is recommended id disease is present

Black.comments: Favors cool, wet weather; over winters on crop debris; more common during periods of high rainfall
Black.management: Harvest infected early to avoid severe infections; rotating crops may reduce incidence of disease

Black.comments: Disease affects English walnut and is widespread in Europe; disease is also known to occur in the Pacific Northwest of the United States
Black.management: Control of anthracnose in pistachio relies on the application of appropriate fungicide sprays; cultural practices can help to reduce the severity of the disease and include: removing leaf debris from around plants, avoiding wetting foliage when irrigating, spacing trees adequately to increase air circulation and applications of nitrogen fertilizer in Spring to delay leaf maturity and reduce the development of lesions

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by moderate temperature, poor air circulation around plant and shady conditions
Black.management: Disease is not severe enough on walnut to warrant control

Black.comments: Disease emergence favored by water saturated soils; disease is usually introduced to orchards through contaminated soil, water and plants
Black.management: Control should focus on minimizing soil wetness and saturation by planting trees in well-draining soils; drainage can be improved by levelling soil or installing drainage systems; avoid wetting tree trunks when irrigating; ensure graft union is several centimeters above the soil line when planting trees

Black.comments: Disease favors cool, humid, rainy environments; prevalent in the early spring and fall
Black.management: Reduce humidity around plants by keeping area free from weeds, using recommended plant spacings and pruning plants; plant gooseberry varieties that are resistant to powdery mildew

Black.comments: Disease emergence is favored by wet weather in Spring; spores are spread by splashing water
Black.management: Remove all leaf debris from around plants as soon as they fall from plants; applications of apropriate fungicide may be necessary

Black.comments: Fungus likely overwinters in infected canes
Black.management: Prune out infected branches in Spring and any wilting canes in Summer; remove plants that are severely infected or dead; protective sprays of appropriate fungicides can help protect uninfected plants from disease

Black.comments: Beetle is a major pest of taro in Papua New Guinea and Fiji
Black.management: The lack of chemical pesticides registered for use on taro beetles means that control relies largely on populations of natural enemies and cultural control methods; taro gardens in Papua New Guinea are often flooded in an attempt to control the pest; farmers may also apply wood ash to the crop

Black.comments: Spread by infected seed; use wide plant spacing to promote air circulation
Black.management: Remove and destroy infected leaves; use wide plant spacing to promote air circulation around foliage

Black.comments: Disease emergence favors cool, rainy weather
Black.management: Plant rhubarb in well draining soils; avoid planting in soils where rhubarb has been grown in the past 4-5 years; mounding the soil can help to promote drainage around the plant

Black.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Black.disease: Cucumber beetles (Western striped cucumber beetle, Western spotted cucumber beetle, Banded cucumber beetle)
Black.comments: Beetles overwinter in soil and leaf litter and emerge from soil when temperatures begin to reach and exceed  12.7°C (55°F)
Black.management: Monitor new planting regularly for signs of beetle; floating row covers can be used to protect the plants from damage but will need to be removed at bloom to allow bees to pollinate plants; applications of kaolin clay can be effective for management of small beetle populations; application of appropriate insecticides may be necessary

Black.management: Destroy all crops residue as soon as possible after harvest or on plant death; apply row covers at planting to protect plants;organically acceptable control methods include applications of insecticidal soaps and oils

Black.management: Avoid planting next to onions, garlic or cereals where very large numbers of thrips can build up; use reflective mulches early in growing season to deter thrips; apply appropriate insecticide if thrips become problematic

Black.comments: Serious disease of glasshouse grown citrus trees; pathogen usually drops to non-damaging levels after tree is transplanted to the field
Black.management: Keep glasshouses well lit and warm during winter to encourage vigorous root growth; use good quality potting soil which provides goo aeration

Black.comments: Symptoms most severe on south facing side of tree exposed to winds
Black.management: In areas where disease is severe, copper fungicides should be applied in Fall and WInter prior to the first rains

Black.comments: Aphids transmit tristeza virus on citrus
Black.management: Aphid numbers tend to naturally decline as leaves harden off but can be a problem on young trees or varieties which continually produce flushes of new growth; pesticides are not generally recommended due to resistance and trees can withstand a high degree of leaf curling

Black.disease: Soft scales (Black scale, Brown soft scale , Citricolla scale)
Black.comments: Insects can produce several overlapping generations per year
Black.management: Organically acceptable methods of control include the application of horticultural oils and preservation of natural enemies

Mushrooms.comments: Fungus survives in dead and decaying wood of infested tree stumps; disease spreads when mycelium colonizes roots of tree
Mushrooms.management: Control of Armillaria root rot once present is extremely difficult; great care should be taken to only plant disease-free planting material; trees should not be planted in recently cleared areas known to have been infested with Armillaria previously; diseased trees, including stumps, should be dug up and removed from orchards; avoid stressing trees by providing adequate irrigation, fertilization and insect control; applications of fungicides is not recommended on infected trees; trees may die within one to several years depending on the severity of the infection

Mushrooms.comments: Growth of the mold is restricted to the casing layer; disease can spread very quickly if left untreated
Mushrooms.management: Control of the disease is achieved through good sanitation practices and cultural control methods; casing should be kept clean and sanitized; all tools and equipment should be cleaned and sanitized between use; keep beds free of any mushroom debris; pathogen in susceptible to low humidity and can be inhibited by lowering the humidity in the growing room

Mushrooms.comments: One of the most common and destructive diseases of cultivated mushrooms
Mushrooms.management: Prevention of disease is best achieved by following good sanitation practices; ensure compost is properly pasteurized prior to use and sterilize any supplements

Mushrooms.comments: May be confused with bacterial infections; spread by flies and/or infected equipment
Mushrooms.management: Bubbles should not be disturbed by touching and can be destroyed using salt; salt should be placed in a plastic cup and then placed over the bubbles to dry them out and provide a barrier to prevent spores spreading; fungicides are available for the treatment of Verticullium diseases of mushrooms but many have toxic effects on the mushrooms as they are themselves a fungus

Mushrooms.comments: Healthy trees are usually infected by infected pieces of wood or tree stumps which have been left in the ground after an orchard is cleared
Mushrooms.management: Disease is difficult to control once it becomes established in an orchard; affected trees showing signs of decline should be removed along with as much of the roots system as possible; area where infected tree was should not be replanted with health citrus for a period of at least one year; fumigating soil can help to reduce soil inoculum but is not always completely effective

Mushrooms.comments: Disease emergence favored by wet soils
Mushrooms.management: Armillaria root rot cannot be effectively controlled once it has become established in an orchard; diseased or dead plants should be uprooted and removed; planting resistant rootstocks is the most effective method of preventing the disease

Crust.comments: Pink disease is a destructive disease of mango grown in the wet tropics
Crust.management: If pink disease is identified the recommended treatment is an application of an appropriate fungicide which can be applied by spraying or painting onto infected bark with a paintbrush

Crust.comments: Spread through infected seed, splashing rain, insects and movement of people between plants; bacterium overwinters in crop debris and can survive for 2.5 years.
Crust.management: Use disease-free seed; do not grow plants in field where cucurbits have been grown in the previous 2 years; protective copper spray may help reduce incidence of disease in warm, humid climates; plant resistant varieties.

Crust.comments: Disease affects many fruit trees in the tropics; infection is unsightly but often harmless
Crust.management: Ensure that trees are properly pruned and fertilized to promote vigor; remove all weeds from around tree bases; employ a wider tree spacing to increase air circulation around the trees; badly infested trees can be treated with copper containing fungicides

Crust.comments: Spread through infected seed, splashing rain, insects and movement of people between plants; bacterium overwinters in crop debris and can survive for 2.5 years
Crust.management: Use disease-free seed; do not grow plants in field where cucurbits have been grown in the previous 2 years; protective copper spray may help reduce incidence of disease in warm, humid climates; plant resistant varieties

Crust.management: Use disease-free seed; do not grow plants in field where cucurbits have been grown in the previous 2 years; protective copper spray may help reduce incidence of disease in warm, humid climates; plant resistant varieties

Crust.management: Use disease-free seed; do not grow plants in field where cucurbits have been grown in the previous 2 years; protective copper spray may help reduce incidence of disease in warm, humid climates; plant resistant varieties

Spots.comments: Fungus survives in mummified fruit on the tree, blighted blossoms, cankers and infected twigs; blossom and twig blights are promoted by periods of wet weather
Spots.management: The currently most effective method of controlling brown rot is through the application of appropriate protective fungicides timed so that they are applied when the susceptible flower parts are exposed or after a wet period; avoiding sprinkler irrigation protects the leaves and flowers from wetness that promotes the disease. Cultural control methods include: removing mummified fruit from tree, pruning infected twigs and reducing plant stress by providing adequate levels of water and fertilizer

Spots.comments: Fungus survives on plant debris
Spots.management: Remove all fallen leaves from orchard in Fall; avoid overhead irrigation; applications of appropriate fungicides may be required; orchards treated for scab are usually free of leaf blight

Spots.disease: Aphids (Green peach aphid, Potato aphid, etc.)
Spots.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Spots.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Spots.comments: Adult insect is a pale green to tan, medium sized moth; insect is also very damaging pests of corn; insect overwinters as pupae in the soil
Spots.management: Monitor plants for eggs and young larvae and also natural enemies that could be damaged by chemicals;

Spots.disease: Cucumber beetles (Western striped cucumber beetle, Western spotted cucumber beetle, Banded cucumber beetle)
Spots.comments: Beetles overwinter in soil and leaf litter and emerge from soil when temperatures begin to reach and exceed  12.7°C (55°F)
Spots.management: Monitor new planting regularly for signs of beetle; floating row covers can be used to protect the plants from damage but will need to be removed at bloom to allow bees to pollinate plants; applications of kaolin clay can be effective for management of small beetle populations; application of appropriate insecticides may be necessary

Spots.comments: Disease emergence favored by high temperature and humidity
Spots.management: Avoid overhead irrigation which can spread the disease; provide adequate space between plants to maximize air circulation around foliage; avoid wounding plants; prune out diseases parts of plants by cutting 6 inches below any visible symptoms; application of appropriate protective fungicides should be made in Spring when old leaves drop from plants

Spots.comments: Endemic to all major tea growing regions
Spots.management: Plant tea varieties which are less susceptible to the disease; apply appropriate foliar or systemic fungicides to protect the plants

Spots.comments: Disease emerges early in Spring during periods of high moisture
Spots.management: Remove all infected flowers from plants; remove all crop debris from around plants; soil drenches with appropriate fungicides can help to reduce the intensity of the disease

Spots.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Spots.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Light infestations can be scraped off the plant and destroyed or infested leaves can be hand picked; heavier infestations can be treated with a horticultural oil after bloom; appropriate insecticides can be applied but are only effective against scales that are actively crawling

Spots.comments: Wet conditions promote growth and spread of disease; spread by rainfall through plant or banana bunch.
Spots.management: Commercially produced fruit should be washed and dipped in fungicide prior to shipping; protect fruit from injury; remove flower parts which can harbour fungus.

Spots.comments: Currently the most important disease of banana; promoted by high moisture and spores spread by wind
Spots.management: Export plantations may require regular fungicide applications; increase plant spacing to improve air circulation and reduce humidity; remove leaves with mature spots

Spots.comments: The disease is favored by hot and humid condition. The pathogen mainly spreads by water splash and wind.
Spots.management: Remove all the infected leaves and burn them. If the disease is severe spray copper based fungicides.

Spots.comments: Currently not known how virus is spread; use disease free crowns to propagate or certified seed
Spots.management: Use only certified planting material; remove and destroy infected plants to limit spread

Spots.comments: Fungus overwinters on leaf debris and on related weeds; disease emergence is favored by moist, warm conditions
Spots.management: Control of disease depends on sanitary practices; treat seeds with hot water prior to planting; rotate crops; plant in an area with good soil drainage; remove all cruciferous weeds which may act as a reservoir for the fungus

Spots.comments: Disease emergence favors cool temperatures and wet weather
Spots.management: Plant only certified disease-free seed; avoid overhead irrigation; rotate crops to non-brassica species for 2-3 years; apply appropriate fungicide if disease emerges

Spots.comments: Disease emergence favors cool temperatures; disease spreads quickly in wet conditions
Spots.management: Remove all crop debris after harvest; rotate with non-brassicas; application of appropriate fungicides may be required if symptoms of disease are present

Spots.comments: Disease emergence favors cool and wet conditions
Spots.management: No known plant resistance to white leaf spot so control relies on cultural practices such as rotating crops and removing weeds; application of appropriate fungicide may help control the disease

Spots.comments: Bacteria overwinter on plant debris; disease emergence is favored by wet weather
Spots.management: Remove plant debris from around plants and after harvest to reduce overwintering inoculum

Spots.comments: Emergence of the disease is favored by periods of cool, wet weather; fungus is spread by splashing water
Spots.management: Infected plant should be removed and destroyed to prevent spread; remove any volunteer plants and weeds; avoid working with plants when they are wet; seeds can be treated with hot water to eliminate the fungus prior to planting

Spots.comments:
The leaf and berry symptoms may occur together in single cane or may show symptom on only one parts. The severe infestation of measles kill grapevine in a single year which is commonly called apoplexy. 
The symptoms are common in 5 to 7 year old vineyard. The prune wounds helps in pathogen entrance and establishment.
Spots.management: Till date there is no effective method to control this disease. Remove the infected berries, leaves and trunk and destroy them. Protect the prune wounds to minimize fungal infection using wound sealant (5% boric acid in acrylic paint) or essential oil or suitable fungicides.

Spots.comments: Disease favors rainy weather; spores may ooze out during rain
Spots.management: Remove all mummified fruit from vines during dormant pruning; cultivate soil during bud break to bury mummies and reduce inoculant; application of appropriate fungicides can help control the disease

Spots.comments: Fungus can enter the plant through propagation wounds
Spots.management: Avoid stressing vines; provide adequate irrigation and do not over-fertilize; do not harvest fruit until vines are at least 3 years old

Spots.comments: Disease favors cool, humid weather
Spots.management: Avoid planting infected sets; rotate crops to non-allium species for 3-4 years; plant in well-draining areas and do not overcrowd plants; destroy all infected crop debris; apply appropriate foliar fungicides taking care to apply thoroughly to waxy leaves

Spots.comments: Disease affects young, tender leaves; disease emergence and spread is favored by prolonged leaf wetness
Spots.management: Plant pathogen-free seed; do not overcrowd plants; rotate crops with non-umbelliferous varieties

Spots.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed; willow-carrot aphid will also attack parnip, parsley and celery
Spots.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Spots.comments: Insect also attacks other crops such as coconut, tea and mango
Spots.management: May be possible to control coconut scale by pruning infested parts of trees and destroying by burning; chemical control may be necessary

Spots.disease: Alternaria leaf spot (Black spot, Gray spot)
Spots.comments: May become a problem during cool, wet periods
Spots.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present

Spots.comments: Fungus overwinters on leaf debris and on related weeds; disease emergence is favored by moist, warm conditions
Spots.management: Control of disease depends on sanitary practices; treat seeds with hot water prior to planting; rotate crops; plant in an area with good soil drainage; remove all cruciferous weeds which may act as a reservoir for the fungus

Spots.comments: Symptoms easily confused with downy mildew; disease emergence favored by wet leaves and cool temperatures
Spots.management: No known plant resistance to white leaf spot so control relies on cultural practices such as rotating crops and removing weeds; application of appropriate fungicide may help control the disease

Spots.comments: Disease can be introduced through infested seed and spread by wind or water splash; symptoms usually occur on younger foliage first
Spots.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; plow crop debris into soil ofter harvest; apply appropriate fungicide sprays

Spots.comments: Disease affects young, tender leaves; disease emergence and spread is favored by prolonged leaf wetness
Spots.management: Plant pathogen-free seed; do not overcrowd plants; rotate crops with non-umbelliferous varieties

Spots.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed; willow-carrot aphid will also attack parnip, carrot and celery
Spots.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Spots.comments: Disease affects many fruit trees in the tropics; infection is unsightly but often harmless
Spots.management: Ensure that trees are properly pruned and fertilized to promote vigor; remove all weeds from around tree bases; employ a wider tree spacing to increase air circulation around the trees; badly infested trees can be treated with copper containing fungicides

Spots.comments: Disease emergence favored by wet conditions; serious disease of mango wherever it is grown
Spots.management: Susceptible mango varieties should be protected with fungicide in commercial production and the timing of the applications are critical to successful control; appropriate fungicide should be applied during flowering and fruit development

Spots.comments: Bacterial black spot is found in most tropical and subtropical areas where mango is grown
Spots.management: Provide windbreaks for plants; prune out infected twigs; protective sprays of copper during wet weather help to protect plants from the disease

Spots.comments: Bacterium overwinters in crop residue; disease more severe when foliage is wet for extended periods
Spots.management: Plant only certified seed; rotate crops regularly; remove crop debris from field after harvest

Spots.comments: Fungus overwinters on plant debris or alternate host; disease emergence is favored by warm, dry weather with cool nights that result in dew formation
Spots.management: Plant resistant varieties, particularly if sowing late; use overhead irrigation (washes fungus from leaves and reduces viability); plant crop as early as possible; frequent applications of sulfur may be required to control heavy infestations

Spots.comments: Disease can be introduced by contaminates seed; bacteria overwinters in crop debris; disease emergence favored by warm temperatures; spread is greatest during humid, wet weather conditions
Spots.management: Plant only certifies seed; plant resistant varieties; treat seeds with an appropriate antibiotic prior to planting to kill off bacteria; spray plants with an appropriate protective copper based fungicide before appearance of symptoms

Spots.disease: Aphids (Pea aphid, Bean aphid, Cowpea aphid, Melon aphid, Peach aphid, etc.)
Spots.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed; transmit viruses
Spots.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Spots.comments: Emergence of the disease is favored by warm, humid cobditions; fungus overwinters on crop debris on the ground
Spots.management: Remove all crop debris after harvest; remove any infected leaves from the plant; apply an appropriate foliar fungicide to control the disease; avoid overhead irrigation; make use of trellises to open up the plat canopy and promote good air circulation around foliage; avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization

Spots.comments: Beetles can decimate bean crops; beetles overwinter as adults and undergo 2-3 generations per year
Spots.management: Some bean varieties may be less attractive hosts for the beetle, e.g. snapbeans are preferred hosts over lima beans; early varieties may escape damage form beetles  beetle populations can be reduced by remove overwintering sites such as brush and leaves on the ground; handpick larvae and adults; brush eggs from leaves and destroy; apply insecticidal soap to leaf undersides if infestation is heavy

Spots.comments: Disease emergence favors warm, wet conditions
Spots.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; applications of appropriate fungicides control disease when present
The collapsed plants are hard to spot in a field and the disease may not be discovered

Spots.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Spots.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Spots.comments: Fungus overwinters on infected leaves on the ground; disease development is promoted by warm temperatures and high humidity
Spots.management: Disease can be controlled through applications of appropriate fungicides, Bordeaux mixture is also effective; no cherry varieties are resistant to leaf spot, plant less susceptible varieties if available

Spots.comments: Fungus overwinters in twigs or in leaves which remain attached to the tree
Spots.management: Rust can be prevented by spraying trees with protective fungicides; application is usually carried out one, two and three months before harvest in areas prone to early season outbreaks of the disease and after harvest in areas where disease is less problematic or emerges later in the season

Spots.comments: Virus can infect a wide range of
Spots.management: No control strategies are currently known; always purchase certified, disease-free material

Spots.comments: Aphids overwinter as eggs on trees and young nymphs hatch and feed at growing tips; winged aphids will migrate to plants in the mustard family during the summer months
Spots.management: Remove any mustard volunteers growing in proximity to cherry; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use; aphids are best treated when cherry is dormant and insect is at the egg stage; if population has not been controlled by the treatment during dormancy then it should be reapplied at petal fall

Spots.comments: Disease occurs in all cocoa growing areas; P. megakarya is most destructive in Central and West Africa whereas P. capsici is most common in Central and South America
Spots.management: Protective sprays of copper containing fungicides in combination with systemic fungicides are often recommended to control the disease; cocoa plants should be well spaced to allow good air circulation through the plantation; mummified pods should be removed and destroyed to reduce spread

Spots.comments: Only occurs in West Africa. Major problem in Togo, Ghana, Cote d'Ivorie and Nigeria; transmitted by mealybugs. The Swollen Shoot Virus is not native to cocoa but jumped into the cocoa from trees that grew in the rain forests of W. Africa (e.g. Cola chlamydanta, Ceiba pentandra, Adansonia digitata, Cola gigantean and Sterculia tragacantha). The virus is a badnavirus within the family Caulimoviridae.
Spots.management: Infected trees and those surrounding them should be removed and destroyed (up to 5 m from infected tree if less than 10 trees are infected and up to a distance of 15 m if greater than 100 trees are infected) to prevent further spread; a gap should be placed between cocoa plantations of at least 10 m (33 ft) and it may be possible to isolate cocoa plantations using a non-host crop such as oil palm growing between plantations.

Spots.disease: Frosty pod (Monilia pod rot, Watery pod rot)
Spots.comments: Causes serious losses in South-Western parts of South America; spores are dry and powdery and are spread easily by water movement, wind or movement of pods; disease spread is highest during periods of high rainfall.
Spots.management: Planting cocoa varieties that produce pods during the dry season allows the pod to avoid the disease; pods showing symptoms of disease should be removed to prevent spread; application of copper containing fungicides will help reduce disease incidence.

Spots.comments: Disease overwinters in diseased crop debris on, or close to, the soil surface; temperatures below 12°C (53.6°F) and above 40°C (104°F) bacterium grows more slowly and may even be killed off.
Spots.management: Plant resistant sweetcorn hybrids; rotate crop; plow crop debris into soil immediately after harvest.

Spots.comments: Disease can occur anywhere where corn is grown but is not usually very damaging.
Spots.management: Disease is usually not severe but if it does become a problem crops should be rotated and any debris should be plowed into the soil after harvest.

Spots.comments: Virus is transmitted by more than 15 different species of aphid and is passed to the plant from the insect in seconds to minutes of feeding; sorghum is also a major host of the virus.
Spots.management: Many commercial corn hybrids are highly tolerant of the disease and no control is needed; control aphid populations on plants and remove any Johnson grass growing in the vicinity as it can act as a reservoir for the virus.

Spots.comments: Adult insect is a pale green to tan, medium sized moth; can be a very damaging pests ofcorn; insect overwinters as pupae in the soil.
Spots.management: Corn earworms are most problematic on sweet corn varieties and treatment should be applied at egg hatch; monitor plants for eggs and young larvae and also natural enemies that could be damaged by chemicals;

Spots.comments: Disease emergence favors cool, wet conditions
Spots.management: The most effective method of controlling rusts is to plant resistant varieties of wheat; other methods of control include: destroying alternate hosts; applications of appropriate protective fungicides; growing wheat varieties that mature early

Spots.comments: Disease can be introduced through infested seed and spread by wind or water splash; symptoms usually occur on younger foliage first
Spots.management: Plant only pathogen-free seed; rotate crops; plow crop debris into soil ofter harvest; apply appropriate fungicide sprays

Spots.comments: Disease spread via infected seed, by contaminated irrigation water and by equipment or people moving through wet plants
Spots.management: Control of disease is reliant on cultural control methods and good sanitation practices: use pathogen free seeds or treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting; rotate crops; plow plant debris into soil; if disease is present avoid working with infected plants when foliage is wet; apply appropriate protective fungicides

Spots.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed; willow-carrot aphid will also attack parnip, carrot and celery
Spots.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Spots.comments: One of the most destructive oat diseases
Spots.management: The most effective method of controlling rusts is to plant resistant varieties of oats; planting oats early allows them to mature before spores reach plants and escape most damage

Spots.comments: Fungus overwinters in in twig lesions or on bark surface
Spots.management: Control currently relies completely on the use of fungicides; pruning the tree canopy promotes good air circulation and allows light to penetrate which can help control scab

Spots.comments: Fungus overwinters in twigs or in leaves which remain attached to the tree
Spots.management: Rust can be prevented by spraying trees with protective fungicides; application is usually carried out one, two and three months before harvest in areas prone to early season outbreaks of the disease and after harvest in areas where disease is less problematic or emerges later in the season

Spots.comments: Virus is transmitted by aphids but most common method of spread is diseased plant material
Spots.management: Plant certified healthy material; remove infected trees from orchard; chemical sprays to control aphids may prolong spread of virus

Spots.comments: Disease emergence is favored by cool, wet weather.
Spots.management: No fungicides are currently registered for use in cotton; plow crop debris into soil after harvest; crop rotation has little to no effect of control of disease.

Spots.comments: The pathogen also infects cucumber, sweet potato, soybean and tomato. It will cause severe loss if pathogen infect at flowering stage. The disease is common in Southeastern cotton producing states of USA. The target spot is confused with leaf spot caused by Cercospora spp. Stemphyllium spp. or Alternaria spp. But this spot is surrounded by reddish to purplish margin.
Spots.management: Use available resistant varieties. Follow crop rotation. Spray suitable fungicide.

Spots.disease: Bacterial blight/angular leaf spot of cotton
Spots.comments: Disease if often introduced to cotton fields by infested seeds.
Spots.management: The use of resistant cotton varieties is the most effective method of controlling the disease; cultural practices such as plowing crop residue into soil after harvest can also limit disease emergence.

Spots.comments: Honeydew excreted by aphids promotes growth of mold. This honeydew also attarcts ants which then protect the aphids from natural enemies and even move aphids to other parts of the plants and even other plants.
Spots.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Spots.comments: Adult insect is a pale green to tan, medium sized moth; insect is also very damaging pests of corn; insect overwinters as pupae in the soil.
Spots.management: Monitor plants for eggs and young larvae and also natural enemies that could be damaged by chemicals; Bacillus thuringiensis or Entrust SC may be applied to control insects on organically grown plants; appropriate chemical treatment may be required for control in commercial. plantations.

Spots.comments: Pseudospora an important disease in China; both diseases occur in Africa
Spots.management: Remove all crop residue from field after harvest; plant disease-free seed

Spots.comments: Can cause complete destruction of canopy
Spots.management: Crop rotation helps to reduce the build up of the fungus in the soil; reduce soil compaction; do not plant seeds too deep
