Soft.comments: The disease is favored by wet condition. The pathogen spreads via water and soil particles.
Soft.management: Avoid excessive soil moisture. Mulching with suitable materials help in preventing the disease.

Soft.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed.
Soft.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Soft.comments: Disease enters through wounds on plant
Soft.management: Only plant disease-free material; plant rosemary in well-draining soils; avoid wounding the plants as much as possible

Soft.comments: Aphids are carried by ants from one plant to another.
Soft.management: Encourage natural enemies. Keep the field free from crop debris and weeds. Spraying strong jet of water to dislodge the aphid. If infestation is severe spray suitable insecticide.

Soft.comments: Disease enters through wounds on plant
Soft.management: Only plant disease-free material; plant sage in well-draining soils; avoid wounding the plants as much as possible

Soft.comments: Black aphids can cause severe damage when infestation is high but more commonly cause problems in chickpea crops through the transmission of several plant viruses
Soft.management: Plants should be monitored for signs of aphids; if insects are present then control measures should be implemented; some chickpea cultivars are more susceptible to aphid infestation than others - cultivars with a low density of trichomes (leaf hairs) are more susceptible than others; chemical control may be warranted although aphids have developed resistance to several insecticides in India; cultural control methods include early planting which promotes early closure of the plant canopy and reduced the spread of viruses; aphid infestations tend to be worse when broad plant spacing is employed

Soft.disease: Aphids (Peach aphid, Potato aphid, Melon aphid, Strawberry aphid)
Soft.comments: Aphids transmit several viruses which can be very damaging to strawberry, the insects rarely reach a high enough population to cause severe damage directly but the spread of viruses is a major concern in strawberry production.
Soft.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Soft.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed; willow-carrot aphid will also attack parnip, carrot and celery.
Soft.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use.

Soft.disease: Aphids (Bird cherry-oat aphid, Russian wheat aphid, Corn leaf aphid, etc.)
Soft.comments: Fields should be checked for aphid populations periodically after emergence
Soft.management: Sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use; in commercial plantations aphid numbers are usually kept in check by predators and natural enemies; beneficial insect populations should be assessed before chemical control is considered; if no beneficial insect populations are present and aphids are damaging then apply appropriate insecticides

Soft.comments: The bacterium enters host plants through wounds and causes plant cells to proliferate and cells to be undifferentiated, leading to the formation of a gall
Soft.management: Only plant disease-free nursery stock; plant trees in well-draining soils; avoid wounding the plants as much as possible; fresh wounds can be treated with a biocontrol agent (

Soft.comments: Aphids overwinter as eggs on stems
Soft.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Soft.comments: Disease emergence appears to favor the presence of warm, stagnant water where cocoyam is grown in wetlands; poor field sanitation practices aid in the spread of the disease
Soft.management: Some varieties are more resistant to the disease than others (e.g. Hawaiian varieties Kai Kea and Kai uliuli) and should be planted if disease is known to be a problem in the area; root rot severity can be reduced in acid soils by the addition of the fungicide Captan prior to planting

Soft.comments: Pink hibiscus mealybugs have a very wide host range and may also be found on other crops such as cassava and mango as well as leguminous plants
Soft.management: Pesticides are often largely ineffective at controlling the insects as they hide in crevices on the plants and avoid contact with the chemical; in the Caribbean, predatory beetles have been very successful at controlling populations of mealy bugs

Soft.comments: Disease emergence favors warm, moist conditions
Soft.management: Efficient management of water to avoid saturated soil; avoid long periods of irrigation

Soft.comments: Distinguishing features include the presence of cornicles (tubular structures) which project backwards from the body of the aphid; will generally not move very quickly when disturbed
Soft.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Soft.comments: Aphids transmit tristeza virus on citrus
Soft.management: Aphid numbers tend to naturally decline as leaves harden off but can be a problem on young trees or varieties which continually produce flushes of new growth; pesticides are not generally recommended due to resistance and trees can withstand a high degree of leaf curling

Mushy.comments: Western cherry fruit flies are a potentially damaging pest in all western statues of the US; high populations can result in a maggot being found in every single fruit
Mushy.management: In commercial cherry plantations, western cherry fruit flies should be monitored using yellow sticky traps, the most effective being one with an ammonium carbonate lure; the insect is a quarantine pest and if a single fruit is found to be infested, treatment is warranted - even if no adults have been found on sticky traps; chemical treatments should be timed to target mature, egg-laying females in your particular region; recommended chemicals include spinosad, carbaryl, malathion, and acetamiprid; home growers should also treat trees to help control this damaging pest

Mushy.comments: Disease is most commonly found in plantations which have overhead irrigation systems or in areas with high rainfall; disease emergence is favored by high temperatures and high humidity.
Mushy.management: Plow all crop debris into soil in Fall; plant corn in well-draining soil to prevent waterlogged plants.

Mushy.comments: Orietal fruit moths overwinter as mature larvae inside protective cocoons in protected areas of trees or in leaf debris on the ground; insect may undergo six or more generations per year
Mushy.management: Management of the oriental fruit moth usually involves the application of insecticides or the use of mating disruptants; commercial growers should monitor moth numbers using pheromone traps and apply insecticide if average number of moths exceeds 10 per trap; chemical sprays should not be applied within 14 days of harvest

Mushy.disease: Fruit flies (Guava fruit fly, Caribbean fruit fly)
Mushy.comments: Guava fruit flies are widespread in Southern Asia while Caribbean fruit flies are damaging pests in Cuba, Jamaica, Hispaniola, Puerto Rico and Florida.
Mushy.management: Infested fruit should be removed and destroyed; plowing around bases of trees infested with guava fruit flies exposes pupae to damaging heat from the sun and to natural enemies; pheromone traps are used successfully in some regions to control guava fruit flies; millions of sterile Caribbean fruit flies are introduced yearly in Florida to control populations on citrus.

Mushy.comments: Disease emergence favors high soil moisture and cool temperatures
Mushy.management: Control of disease is dependent on minimizing soil moisture: break up compacted soil; plant in well-draining areas or raised beds; treat seeds with appropriate fungicides prior to planting

Mushy.comments: Bacteria may be present on leaves but do not cause damage unless plant is stressed or bacteria enter the plant through a wound
Mushy.management: No treatment for disease; use certified seed; disinfected tools and cuttings; employ crop rotation; remove plant debris from soil

Mushy.comments: Disease favors warm, moist soils; spread primarily through use of infected seed pieces which may not show any outward signs of disease.
Mushy.management: Plant ginger in well-draining soils or on hills created by tilling; do not plant any seed pieces which show symptoms of disease; seed pieces can be treated with hot water (50°C/122°F for 10 min) or appropriate fungicides prior to planting; destroy all crop debris after harvest; keep fields weed free; do not grow ginger for more than one year in same area.

Mushy.comments: Disease emergence favors high soil moisture and cool temperatures
Mushy.management: Control of disease is dependent on minimizing soil moisture: break up compacted soil; plant in well-draining areas or raised beds; treat seeds with appropriate fungicides prior to planting

Mushy.comments: Disease emergence appears to favor the presence of warm, stagnant water where cocoyam is grown in wetlands; poor field sanitation practices aid in the spread of the disease
Mushy.management: Some varieties are more resistant to the disease than others (e.g. Hawaiian varieties Kai Kea and Kai uliuli) and should be planted if disease is known to be a problem in the area; root rot severity can be reduced in acid soils by the addition of the fungicide Captan prior to planting

Spores.comments: Disease is of economic importance in Central and West Africa; it also occurs in India, Iran, South Africa, South America, the Canaries and the West Indies.
Spores.management: Infected flowers should be removed from the plant; bunches should be bagged using perforated polyethylene; chemical control may be necessary in the case of severe infestations.

Spores.comments: Emergence of the disease is favored by warm, humid cobditions; fungus overwinters on crop debris on the ground
Spores.management: Remove all crop debris after harvest; remove any infected leaves from the plant; apply an appropriate foliar fungicide to control the disease; avoid overhead irrigation; make use of trellises to open up the plat canopy and promote good air circulation around foliage; avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization

Spores.comments: Fungus can survive for long periods of time in dry conditions; disease spread by wind
Spores.management: Rotate crops; plant only disease-free seed; apply appropriate fungicide if disease becomes a problem

Spores.comments: Fungus overwinters in twigs or in leaves which remain attached to the tree
Spores.management: Rust can be prevented by spraying trees with protective fungicides; application is usually carried out one, two and three months before harvest in areas prone to early season outbreaks of the disease and after harvest in areas where disease is less problematic or emerges later in the season

Spores.comments: Disease is spread by wind-borne spores; some of the most popularly grown sweet corn varieties have little or no resistance to the disease.
Spores.management: The most effective method of controlling the disease is to plant resistant hybrids; application of appropriate fungicides may provide some degree on control and reduce disease severity; fungicides are most effective when the amount of secondary inoculum is still low, generally when plants only have a few rust pustules per leaf.

Spores.comments: Fungus overwinters on crop debris or in the soil and can survive for several years; fungus usually enters the plant through wounds; application of nitrogen fertilizer increases incidence of disease, while application of phosphorous fertilizer decreases infection.
Spores.management: Although many practices may be recommended for the control of common smut, the only method that is completely effective is to grow resistant corn hybrids.

Spores.comments: The disease mainly spread through rain splash and wind.
Spores.management: Follow proper tillage to reduce fungus inoculum from crop debris. Follow crop rotation with non host crop. Grow available resistant varieties. In severe case of disease incidence apply suitable fungicide.

Spores.comments: Disease is most commonly introduced through infected seed although spores are spread by wind
Spores.management: Disease can be controlled by planting resistant wheat varieties, planting disease-free seed and using a seed treatment prior to planting; disease may also be avoided by planting wheat early in the Fall and by shallow seeding

Spores.comments: Fungus overwinters in twigs or in leaves which remain attached to the tree
Spores.management: Rust can be prevented by spraying trees with protective fungicides; application is usually carried out one, two and three months before harvest in areas prone to early season outbreaks of the disease and after harvest in areas where disease is less problematic or emerges later in the season

Spores.comments: Pseudospora an important disease in China; both diseases occur in Africa
Spores.management: Remove all crop residue from field after harvest; plant disease-free seed

Spores.comments: Disease emergence favors cool dry soils
Spores.management: Disease can be controlled by growing resistant varieties and through the application of appropriate fungicides

Spores.comments: Disease emergence favors warm, wet weather; spread easily during wet weather by water splash
Spores.management: The primary method of controlling the disease is to plant resistant guava cultivars; both systemic and non-systemic fungicides are effective at controlling the disease and are usually applied shortly before flowering and during fruit develpment

Spores.comments: Fungus requires two hosts to complete lifecycle; forms galls on Eastern red cedar and spores are carried by wind to apple; use caution when planting apple close to red cedar.
Spores.management: Plant resistant varieties where possible; remove nearby red cedar; if growing susceptible varieties in proximity to red cedar follow a fungicide program.

Spores.comments: Disease emergence occurs when high temperatures coincide with periods of high humidity
Spores.management: Avoid planting millet varieties that are highly susceptible to Cercospora fungi; control weeds in field; rotate crops and practice good sanitation; no chemical control is needed to treat the disease

Spores.comments: Disease emergence occurs when high temperatures coincide with periods of high humidity.
Spores.management: Avoid planting millet varieties that are highly susceptible to Cercospora fungi; control weeds in field; rotate crops and practice good sanitation; no chemical control is needed to treat the disease.

Spores.comments: Biology and epidemiology: Individual florets are infected by air-borne spores of the causal fungus when the inflorescences are at the protogynous stage. i.e. when the stigmas have emerged before pollination has occurred. Mature spores released from the sori when the crop is threshed are mixed with seed from non-infected florets, and these are the source of infection for the subsequent crop.
Spores.management: Smut is best managed by the use of resistant cultivars.

Spores.comments: Peanut rust is highly specific to peanut; disease emergence and spread is favored by warm temperatures followed by leaf wetness.
Spores.management: Allow field to fallow for at least one month between successive peanut plantings; remove any volunteer peanut plants during fallowing to reduce inoculum; sprays of appropriate fungicides such as Bordeaux mixture can effectively control the disease; such fungicides are often also effective at controlling leaf spot.

Spores.comments: Fungus overwinters in dried up remains of fruit
Spores.management: Primary method of controlling cottonball is the application of appropriate fungicides if available; cultural practices that reduce incidence of the disease have not yet been investigated

Spores.comments: Fungi spread via wind and rain and enter plant via wounds
Spores.management: Avoid wounding fruits during harvest

Spores.comments: Gray mold is usually associated with wounds on plants; can develop in relatively cool conditions.
Spores.management: Liming soil to increase calcium content can help to reduce plant susceptibility to gray mold; application of appropriate fungicide prior to formation of dense canopy.

Spores.comments: Disease favors cool, humid weather
Spores.management: Do not overcrowd plants; avoid overhead irrigation, water plants from base; apply appropriate fungicide

Spores.comments: Emergence of Botrytis fruit rot is favored by cool and wet conditions; physical damage to fruit increases likelihood of infection.
Spores.management: Promote air circulation around vines by using trellises or training the vines; avoid over fertilizing plants; protective fungicides can be used to control the disease and should be applied at intervals of 7-14 days from early bloom right through to harvest.

Spores.comments: Disease common during wet Springs or long periods of wet weather late in season
Spores.management: If disease is damaging then appropriate fungicides should be applied to whole tree

Spores.comments: Disease common during wet Springs or long periods of wet weather late in season
Spores.management: If disease is damaging then appropriate fungicides should be applied to whole tree

Spores.comments: Fungus overwinters in twigs or in leaves which remain attached to the tree
Spores.management: Rust can be prevented by spraying trees with protective fungicides; application is usually carried out one, two and three months before harvest in areas prone to early season outbreaks of the disease and after harvest in areas where disease is less problematic or emerges later in the season

Spores.comments: Disease emergence favors warm temperatures.
Spores.management: If disease is damaging then appropriate fungicides should be applied to whole tree.

Spores.comments: Fungus overwinters in twigs or in leaves which remain attached to the tree.
Spores.management: Rust can be prevented by spraying trees with protective fungicides; application is usually carried out one, two and three months before harvest in areas prone to early season outbreaks of the disease and after harvest in areas where disease is less problematic or emerges later in the season.

Spores.comments: Disease common during wet Springs or long periods of wet weather late in season.
Spores.management: If disease is damaging then appropriate fungicides should be applied to whole tree.

Spores.comments: Fungus mainly attacks plants that have been damaged by insects or by mechanical means; spread by air currents and via infected seed; disease emergence favors warm, moist conditions
Spores.management: Plant varieties resistant to disease; only use certified seed; do not plant crop densely; treat disease with copper fungicides if it emerges

Spores.comments: Symptoms all occur below ground
Spores.management: Do not plant tubers showing symptoms of disease; avoid planting potato in poorly draining soils; if disease occurs rotate crop away from potato for a period of 3-10 years and avoid planting tomato

Spores.comments: Disease emergence favors cool, wet conditions.
Spores.management: Avoid over fertilizing tobacco crop and the use of overhead irrigation which created favorable conditions for the development of the fungus; applications of appropriate protective fungicides is usually necessary to control the disease in temperate and subtropical areas.

Spores.comments: The disease is favored by wet weather conditions. The pathogen is more prevalent in Taiwan, Nigeria, Pakistan, Central America and India.
Spores.management: Remove and burn the infected leaves. Follow crop rotation. Keep the field free from weeds. Follow proper crop density. If the disease is severe apply suitable fungicides.

Spores.comments: The pathogen overwinters on the seed, in crop debris and in soil.
Spores.management: Remove and destroy the infected leaves and plant debris. Keep the field free from weeds. Use disease free seeds. Grow available resistant varieties. Follow crop rotation. Spray with suitable fungicides.

Spores.comments: Fungi over winter in plant debris.
Spores.management: Rot can be minimized by picking fruit before it becomes overripe; reducing dust in orchards may also help to reduce the incidence of rot.

Spores.comments: Disease emergence favors water-stressed trees.
Spores.management: Avoid stressing trees by providing adequate irrigation; reducing dust in the orchard may help to reduce incidence of rots.

Spores.comments: Disease emergence favors wet, cool springs.
Spores.management: Infected areas of trees should be pruned out beginning just below the canker.

Spores.comments: Fungus is transferred to fruit by some species of fly, thrips and beetle; fig varieties with smaller ostioles (pores) on the fruit are less susceptible to the disease than those with large ostioles.
Spores.management: Remove all old fruit an crop debris from orchard; try to reduce dust around trees.

Spores.comments: Disease common during wet Springs or long periods of wet weather late in season
Spores.management: If disease is damaging then appropriate fungicides should be applied to whole tree

Rotting.comments:
The leaf and berry symptoms may occur together in single cane or may show symptom on only one parts. The severe infestation of measles kill grapevine in a single year which is commonly called apoplexy. 
The symptoms are common in 5 to 7 year old vineyard. The prune wounds helps in pathogen entrance and establishment.
Rotting.management: Till date there is no effective method to control this disease. Remove the infected berries, leaves and trunk and destroy them. Protect the prune wounds to minimize fungal infection using wound sealant (5% boric acid in acrylic paint) or essential oil or suitable fungicides.

Rotting.comments: Fungus survives in soil and disease emergence is favored by moist to wet soil
Rotting.management: Plant only disease-free seed; treat seed with fungicide; rotate crops with cereals or grasses to reduce levels of pathogen in soil; steam treatment or fumigation of soil can help reduce levels of Fusarium in the soil

Rotting.comments: Damping-off diseases favor conditions which slow seed germination; fungi can be spread in water, contaminated soil or on equipment
Rotting.management: Avoid planting parsnips in poorly draining, cool, wet soil; planting in raised beds will help with soil drainage; plant high quality seed that germinates quickly; treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting to eliminate fungal pathogens

Rotting.disease: Watery soft rot (White mold, Cabbage drop)
Rotting.comments: Disease emergence is favored by frequent rainfall that keep soil close to saturation
Rotting.management: The number of sclerotia in the soil can be reduced by plowing crop debris deep into soil and rotating crops every 3 years with non-host crops; severe infestations may require control through application of appropriate fungicide

Rotting.comments: Disease emergence favors light, sandy soils
Rotting.management: The most effective method of controlling the disease is through the use of resistant varieties of sweet potato; if resistant varieties are not being used then the soil should be maintained at a low pHwhich is unfavorable to the pathogen; sweet potato should be rotated with other crops which are non-hosts to prevent build  up of the pathogen in the soil; fumiigation of the soil prior to planting can be an effective method of reducing the severity of the disease

Rotting.comments: Damping-off diseases favor conditions which slow seed germination; fungi can be spread in water, contaminated soil or on equipment
Rotting.management: Avoid planting parsley in poorly draining, cool, wet soil; planting in raised beds will help with soil drainage; plant high quality seed that germinates quickly; treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting to eliminate fungal pathogens

Rotting.comments: Damping-off diseases favor conditions which slow seed germination; fungi can be spread in water, contaminated soil or on equipment
Rotting.management: Avoid planting in poorly draining, cool, wet soil; planting in raised beds will help with soil drainage; plant high quality seed that germinates quickly; treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting to eliminate fungal pathogens

Rotting.comments: Surface damage caused to fruit by female laying her eggs; larvae damage flesh by burrowing and feeding.
Rotting.management: Use red spherical sticky traps to trap adults, place one trap for every 100 apple fruits; bag apples by tying or stapling polythene bags around fruit to prevent adults laying eggs - cut corners from bags to ensure air supply to fruit; spray fruit with insecticide prior to eggs being laid.

Rotting.comments: Generally occurs wherever barley is grown but is more common in water stressed plants.
Rotting.management: No chemical treatments for this disease; plant crop in late fall to avoid warm soils which favor emergence of disease; do not fertilize crop excessively; use irrigation to reduce water stress.

Rotting.comments: Disease outbreaks sporadic but can be very damaging
Rotting.management: Appropriate fungicides should be applied at first sign of infection to reduce the severity of the infection; cultural control methods include thinning the canopy to promote good air circulation around the foliage and planting pecan in well-draining soils

Rotting.comments: Fungi enter the trunk through wounds.
Rotting.management: Avoid wounding palms with machinery and tools to reduce disease incidence; disease can be controlled with applications of the fungicide benomyl where registered; infected trees should be removed and destroyed as soon as possible.

Rotting.comments: Damping-off diseases favor conditions which slow seed germination; fungi can be spread in water, contaminated soil or on equipment
Rotting.management: Avoid planting celery in poorly draining, cool, wet soil; planting in raised beds will help with soil drainage; plant high quality seed that germinates quickly; treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting to eliminate fungal pathogens

Rotting.comments: Disease emergence favored by wet, poorly-draining soils; fungus persists in the soil and becomes active during the monsoon season
Rotting.management: Plants must be protected during pre-monsoon period by removing all crop debris including diseased plants and burning; try to reduce the amount of shade on the plantation and promote good drainage

Rotting.comments: Root rot disease emergence is often favored by waterlogged, poorly-draining soils
Rotting.management: Plant cassava in well-draining soils; remove and destroy all crop debris by burning; sanitize all tools after use

Rotting.comments: Fungus overwinters in bark, dead leaves and rotting fruit; disease incidence is increased when raking is used during wet harvest of cranberries
Rotting.management: Disease usually manifests as a post harvest berry rot and only requires control if berries are for soft market; fungicides are available in some US states; end rot development can be slowed by refrigerating berries after harvest

Rotting.disease: Watery soft rot (White rot, Cabbage drop)
Rotting.comments: Disease emergence is favored by frequent rainfall that keep soil close to saturation
Rotting.management: The number of sclerotia in the soil can be reduced by plowing crop debris deep into soil and rotating crops every 3 years with non-host crops; severe infestations may require control through application of appropriate fungicide

Rotting.comments: Disease is spread via movement of infested soil; bacteria survive in the soil on plant debris.
Rotting.management: Plant ginger in well draining soils where ginger had not previously been grown; plant only pathogen free seed; plant ginger on hills to aid soil drainage and promote air flow around the rhizome; rotate ginger with non-hosts of bacterial wilt.

Rotting.comments: Galls or lesions can appear as quickly as a month prior to planting; nematodes prefer sandy soils and damage in the areas of the field or garden with this type of soil is most likely.
Rotting.management: Plant resistant varieties if nematodes are known to be present in the soil ;check roots of plants mid-season or sooner if symptoms indicate nematodes; solarizing soil can reduce nematode populations in the soil and levels of inoculum of many other pathogens. Treating rhizome with hot water (51°C for 10 minutes) before planting reduces burrowing nematode problem.

Rotting.comments: Disease emergence in seedlings favored by cool temperatures
Rotting.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Rotting.comments: Most important disease of rice worldwide; causes most damage in areas of intense cultivation; disease emergence favors high soil nitrogen content
Rotting.management: If disease is not endemic to the region, blast can be controlled by planting resistant rice varieties; avoid over-fertilizing crop with nitrogen as this increases the plant's susceptibility to the disease; utilize good water management to ensure plants do not suffer from drought stress; disease can be effectively controlled by the application of appropriate systemic fungicides, where available

Rotting.comments: Fungus survives in soil and disease emergence is favored by moist to wet soil
Rotting.management: Plant only disease-free seed; treat seed with fungicide; rotate crops with cereals or grasses to reduce levels of pathogen in soil; steam treatment or fumigation of soil can help reduce levels of Fusarium in the soil

Rotting.comments: Damage to plants by bulb mites allows secondary invasion by other pathogens and can cause bulb rots
Rotting.management: Do not plant successive crops of onion or garlic in same location; allow field to fallow to ensure that any residual organic matter decomposes completely - crop residues can harbor mite populations; treating garlic seed cloves with hot water prior to planting may help reduce mite populations

Rotting.comments: Damping-off diseases favor conditions which slow seed germination; fungi can be spread in water, contaminated soil or on equipment
Rotting.management: Avoid planting dill in poorly draining, cool, wet soil; planting in raised beds will help with soil drainage; plant high quality seed that germinates quickly; treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting to eliminate fungal pathogens

Rotting.comments: Disease emergence favored by heavy rainfall and poorly draining, waterlogged soils
Rotting.management: Do not plant in poorly draining soils; avoid over-watering plants; rotate cucurbits with non-susceptible plants for a period of at least 3 years

Rotting.comments: Healthy trees are usually infected by infected pieces of wood or tree stumps which have been left in the ground after an orchard is cleared
Rotting.management: Disease is difficult to control once it becomes established in an orchard; affected trees showing signs of decline should be removed along with as much of the roots system as possible; area where infected tree was should not be replanted with health citrus for a period of at least one year; fumigating soil can help to reduce soil inoculum but is not always completely effective

Rotting.comments: Healthy trees are usually infected by infected pieces of wood or tree stumps which have been left in the ground after an orchard is cleared.
Rotting.management: Disease is difficult to control once it becomes established in an orchard; affected trees showing signs of decline should be removed along with as much of the roots system as possible; area where infected tree was should not be replanted with health citrus for a period of at least one year; fumigating soil can help to reduce soil inoculum but is not always completely effective.

Rotting.comments: Disease is transmitted by planthoppers
Rotting.management: Disease severity can be reducced by injecting the antibiotic oxytetracycline HCl into the trunk; antibiotics can also be administered as a protective measure if the disease is known to be in the area; control of the disease long term relies on planting resistant or tolerant varieties

Rotting.comments: Disease favors warm weather and very wet soil with poor drainage
Rotting.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Rotting.comments: Disease emergence in seedlings favored by cool temperatures and waterlogged soils
Rotting.management: Plant pathogen-free seed or transplants that have been produced in sterilized soil; apply fungicide to seed to kill off any fungi; shallow plant seeds or delay planting until soil warms

Rotting.comments: Damage to plants by bulb mites allows secondary invasion by other pathogens and can cause bulb rots
Rotting.management: Do not plant successive crops of onion or garlic in same location; allow field to fallow to ensure that any residual organic matter decomposes completely - crop residues can harbor mite populations; treating garlic seed cloves with hot water prior to planting may help reduce mite populations

Rotting.comments: Occurs more often in cold temperatures when growth of seedlings is slow and in moist soil
Rotting.management: Treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting

Rotting.comments: Healthy trees are usually infected by infected pieces of wood or tree stumps which have been left in the ground after an orchard is cleared.
Rotting.management: Disease is difficult to control once it becomes established in an orchard; affected trees showing signs of decline should be removed along with as much of the roots system as possible; area where infected tree was should not be replanted with health citrus for a period of at least one year; fumigating soil can help to reduce soil inoculum but is not always completely effective.

Rotting.comments: Disease only affects tubers and fungus can only enter through wounds; all common potato cultivars are susceptible to leak; disease emergence is favored by relatively high temperatures
Rotting.management: Rotating crops away from potato and destroying any infected tubers helps to control the disease; infection can be reduced by application of appropriate foliar fungicides; delaying harvest to allow the skin of the tuber to mature reduced the risk of injury to the tuber

Rotting.comments: Disease favors warm, humid conditions.
Rotting.management: Till soil deeply prior to planting; use plastic mulch to create a barrier between fruit and soil; plant in sites with good drainage to avoid wet soils; apply appropriate protective fungicides when plants begin to vine.

Rotting.comments: Damping-off diseases favor conditions which slow seed germination; fungi can be spread in water, contaminated soil or on equipment
Rotting.management: Avoid planting carrots in poorly draining, cool, wet soil; planting in raised beds will help with soil drainage; plant high quality seed that germinates quickly; treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting to eliminate fungal pathogens

Rotting.comments: Disease found in Hawaii.
Rotting.management: Dipping fruits in hot water at 48°C for 20 minutes reduces the incidence of the disease and is currently the only method of control.

Rotting.comments: Disease favors warm, moist soil.
Rotting.management: Plant fungicide treated seed; rotate crops on 4 year rotation.

Rotting.comments: Rain and overhead irrigation helps in spreading pathogen from plant to plant.
Rotting.management: Use disease free seed materials. Follow crop rotation. Spray suitable fungicide.

Rotting.comments: May be linked to moisture levels at time of flowering.
Rotting.management: No known method of control.

Rotting.comments: Insect lay eggs on weeds near the field in the off season.
Rotting.management: Keep the field free from weeds. Remove the infected fruits and burn them. Use pheromone trap to kill adult insects.

Rotting.comments: Occurs more often in cold temperatures when growth of seedlings is slow and in moist soil
Rotting.management: Treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting

Rotting.comments: The pathogen survive on weeds like pigweed, Kochia, and lambs quarters during off season.
Rotting.management: Plant resistant varieties. Crop rotation with non host crop. Keep field free from weeds.

Rotting.disease: Gray mold (Botrytis fruit rot, Blossom blight)
Rotting.comments: Disease emergence favored by extended periods of high humidity or leaf wetness during flowering and moderate temperatures.
Rotting.management: Remove and destroy all dead or infected material; remove decaying fruit; grow fruit under plastic; use plastic mulch to reduce fruit contact with soil; apply appropriate fungicides; plant in areas where wind will rapidly dry wet plants and fruit; plow crop debris into soil after harvest.

Rotting.comments: Disease emergence favors warm, moist conditions
Rotting.management: Efficient management of water to avoid saturated soil; avoid long periods of irrigation

Rotting.comments: Healthy trees are usually infected by infected pieces of wood or tree stumps which have been left in the ground after an orchard is cleared
Rotting.management: Disease is difficult to control once it becomes established in an orchard; affected trees showing signs of decline should be removed along with as much of the roots system as possible; area where infected tree was should not be replanted with health citrus for a period of at least one year; fumigating soil can help to reduce soil inoculum but is not always completely effective

Residue.comments: Large populations of soft scale promote the development of sooty mold
Residue.management: Natural enemies of the soft scale can generally provide good control of the pest; application of horticultural oil may be applied if scales become problematic; healthy plants are less susceptible to scale damage than those which are stressed so adequate irrigation and fertilization are important in scale management

Residue.comments: Insect emergence favored by shady and humid conditions
Residue.management:
1. Collect and burn fallen leaves and twigs.
2. Flooding orchard with water during October kills egg present in soil. Also deep ploughing in November exposes egg to sunlight.
3. After hatching the nymphs start climbing tree and suck sap. To avoid this band the tree trunks with polythene sheet (400 gauge, 30 cm wide) at a height of about 30 cm from the ground level and apply grease at the lower edge of band. Or you can use Funnel Type Slippery Traps.
4. To control insects already on tree you can spray fish oil rosin soap or azadirachtin (neem products).
5. Also soil application of the spores of the fungus, Beauveria bassiana helps in reducing mealybug population.
6. If infestation is severe you can spray suitable insecticides

Residue.comments: Disease overwinters in diseased crop debris on, or close to, the soil surface; temperatures below 12°C (53.6°F) and above 40°C (104°F) bacterium grows more slowly and may even be killed off.
Residue.management: Plant resistant sweetcorn hybrids; rotate crop; plow crop debris into soil immediately after harvest.

Residue.comments: Aphids overwinter as eggs on stems
Residue.management: If aphid population is limited to just a few leaves or shoots then the infestation can be pruned out to provide control; check transplants for aphids before planting; use tolerant varieties if available; reflective mulches such as silver colored plastic can deter aphids from feeding on plants; sturdy plants can be sprayed with a strong jet of water to knock aphids from leaves; insecticides are generally only required to treat aphids if the infestation is very high - plants generally tolerate low and medium level infestation; insecticidal soaps or oils such as neem or canola oil are usually the best method of control; always check the labels of the products for specific usage guidelines prior to use

Yellow Vein.comments: Disease can develop rapidly in moist, cool conditions; spread by water splash
Yellow Vein.management: Only plant disease-free nursery stock; plant trees in well-draining soil and avoid injuries to bark on trunk; trunk wraps can provide protection from freezing

Yellow Vein.comments: Disease can develop rapidly in moist, cool conditions; spread by water splash
Yellow Vein.management: Only plant disease-free nursery stock; plant trees in well-draining soil and avoid injuries to bark on trunk; trunk wraps can provide protection from freezing

Yellow Vein.comments: Disease can develop rapidly in moist, cool conditions; spread by water splash
Yellow Vein.management: Only plant disease-free nursery stock; plant trees in well-draining soil and avoid injuries to bark on trunk; trunk wraps can provide protection from freezing

Greasy.comments: Bacteria survive on crop debris; disease emergence favored by warm temperatures and wet weather; symptoms are very similar to other tomato diseases but only bacterial spot will cause a cut leaf to ooze bacterial exudate; the disease is spread by infected seed, wind-driven rain, diseased transplants, or infested soil; bacteria enter the plant through any natural openings on the leaves or any openings caused by injury to the leaves.
Greasy.management: Use only certified seed and healthy transplants; remove all crop debris from planting area; do not use sprinkler irrigation, instead water from base of plant; rotate crops.
Plant resistant varieties; if signs of disease are present or if rainy conditions are likely or if using overhead irrigation appropriate fungicides should be applied.

Greasy.comments: Warm, wet weather favors disease emergence; beet very susceptible
Greasy.management: Treat seeds with fungicide prior to planting; plant in well draining soil; do not plant until soil is sufficiently warm

Greasy.comments: Little is known about the survival and spread of the pathogen which causes the disease; spores are believed to be spread by splashing water; disease will develop rapidly during periods of moderate to warm weather with high rainfall
Greasy.management: Plant high quality seed which is free of diseases; rotate crop away from sunflower for a period of 3 years, especially if overhead irrigation is used; fungicides are rarely required for the treatment of Septoria leaf spot

Oily.comments: Serious disease in humid tropical and subtropical areas; disease spreads rapidly over short distances.
Oily.management: Control of the disease is reliant on timely sprays of copper containing fungicides and the provision of windbreaks to reduce spread of inoculum from infected trees.

Circular.comments: Fungus can survive in soil for in excess of 5 years; disease can be spread by wind, contaminated irrigation water and by infected seeds
Circular.management: Rotate crops with non-hosts like cereals and corn; plant rows parallel to direction of prevailing winds to prevent spread of disease from secondary hosts nearby; avoid excessive nitrogen fertilizer; use a wide row spacing

Circular.management: Control of disease depends on sanitary practices; treat seeds with hot water prior to planting; rotate crops; plant in an area with good soil drainage; remove all cruciferous weeds which may act as a reservoir for the fungus

Circular.management: Plant only certified disease-free seed; avoid overhead irrigation; rotate crops to non-brassica species for 2-3 years; apply appropriate fungicide if disease emerges

Circular.comments: Mango tree borers are a pest of mango trees in many parts of Asia, Africa, Central America, the Caribbean and the Solomon Islands; female tree borers lay their eggs in an incision made in damaged mango bark; larvae bore through the wood as they feed and eventually pupate within the tree; adult insects emerge from an exit hole that they cut in the wood. adult insects are 25–55 mm long with distinctive long antennae which extend the length of the body
Circular.management: Application of appropriate insecticide to the trunk and branches of the tree when adult insects are present acts to kill any eggs and larvae that are present; insecticide applied to growing twigs and green shoots may deter adult feeding; probing injury sites with a knife or piece of wire can help to destroy larvae and eggs

Circular.comments: Disease can be introduced by contaminates seed; bacteria overwinters in crop debris; disease emergence favored by warm temperatures; spread is greatest during humid, wet weather conditions
Circular.management: Plant only certifies seed; plant resistant varieties; treat seeds with an appropriate antibiotic prior to planting to kill off bacteria; spray plants with an appropriate protective copper based fungicide before appearance of symptoms

Circular.comments: Disease emergence favors wet weather; disease stubble from previously infested crop is the main source of inoculum
Circular.management: Use of resistant varieties is very important in the control of the disease; pathogen varies in virulence throughout the world and rapeseed varieties therefore differ in susceptibility based on geographic location; rapeseed should be planted on a 3 year rotation to promote plant vigor and reduce disease susceptibility; crop stubb;e should be plowed into soil

Circular.comments: Infection of fruit is more likely if there are rainfalls close to harvest
Circular.management: Management of powdery mildew in cherry is reliant on the application of appropriate fungicides and cultural practices which promote good air circulation around tree canopies to lower humidity

Circular.comments: Disease can occur anywhere where corn is grown but is not usually very damaging.
Circular.management: Disease is usually not severe but if it does become a problem crops should be rotated and any debris should be plowed into the soil after harvest.

Circular.comments: Rot continues to develop in stored tubers
Circular.management: Only disease-free seed material should be planted; sweet potato should not be planted in sites where sweet potato has been grown during the previous 3-4 years; transplant material should be collected from plant by making cuts above-ground; seed material should be treated with an appropriate fungicide prior to planting

Circular.comments: Disease emergence favors light, sandy soils
Circular.management: The most effective method of controlling the disease is through the use of resistant varieties of sweet potato; if resistant varieties are not being used then the soil should be maintained at a low pHwhich is unfavorable to the pathogen; sweet potato should be rotated with other crops which are non-hosts to prevent build  up of the pathogen in the soil; fumiigation of the soil prior to planting can be an effective method of reducing the severity of the disease

Circular.comments: Fungus overwinters in in twig lesions or on bark surface
Circular.management: Control currently relies completely on the use of fungicides; pruning the tree canopy promotes good air circulation and allows light to penetrate which can help control scab

Circular.comments: Plants stressed by drought, nutrient deficiency and other pests are more susceptible to the disease; fungus spreads rapidly in dense canopies, especially during periods of warm, wet weather.
Circular.management: Plow crop residue into the soil to reduce inoculum levels; provide plants with adequate irrigation and nutrients, particularly potassium; applications of appropriate foliar fungicides may be required on susceptible cultivars.

Circular.comments: Fungus overwinters in crop debris from previous growing season; commonly found alongside Alternaria leaf spot and other foliar disease.
Circular.management: Plow crop residue into the soil to reduce inoculum levels; provide plants with adequate irrigation and nutrients; applications of appropriate foliar fungicides may be required on susceptible cultivars.

Circular.comments: The pathogen also infects cucumber, sweet potato, soybean and tomato. It will cause severe loss if pathogen infect at flowering stage. The disease is common in Southeastern cotton producing states of USA. The target spot is confused with leaf spot caused by Cercospora spp. Stemphyllium spp. or Alternaria spp. But this spot is surrounded by reddish to purplish margin.
Circular.management: Use available resistant varieties. Follow crop rotation. Spray suitable fungicide.

Circular.comments: Pseudospora an important disease in China; both diseases occur in Africa
Circular.management: Remove all crop residue from field after harvest; plant disease-free seed

Circular.comments: Can cause complete destruction of canopy
Circular.management: Crop rotation helps to reduce the build up of the fungus in the soil; reduce soil compaction; do not plant seeds too deep

Circular.management: Plant high quality seed and use resistant varieties; rotate crop away from soybean for a period of 2 years; treat seeds with appropriate fungicide prior to planting; apply appropriate foliar fungicide

Circular.comments: Disease emergence favors warm temperatures
Circular.management: Plant resistant varieties; remove other susceptible plants e.g. Johnson grass; rotate crops; plow crop debris into soil after harvest

Circular.comments: Fungus may be able to spread via infested seed; common disease of sorghum during wet weather; fungus also attacks millet speacies
Circular.management: Losses can be reduced by rotating crop and practice of good sanitation by removing crop debris and susceptible weed species from the field

Circular.comments: Infection of leaves occurs during wet conditions when temperatures are between 13 and 25°C (55-77°F); disease can be spread by splashing water.
Circular.management: In areas where environmental conditions are conducive to the development of the disease, chemical control using appropriate fungicides is necessary to control the disease; copper-containing fungicides are most effective.

Circular.comments: Disease emergence favored by warm temperatures and high humidity.
Circular.management: Primary method of controlling disease is usually the application of appropriate fungicides; cultural practices that may reduce the incidence of the disease include good sanitation practices and adequate fertilization, irrigation and pruning of trees.

Circular.comments: Spores can overwinter in twigs or fruit remaining on the tree and spread during rainfall.
Circular.management: Remove dead wood, mummified fruit and cankers from trees to reduce spread of disease; burn any prunings that have been made from the tree; disease can be controlled by applying fungicides from silver tip to harvest.

Circular.comments: Outbreaks usually follow above average summer temperatures and frequent rainfall.
Circular.management: Prune trees to open canopy and promote drying of fruit surface; fungicides may be applied as a preventative measure.

Circular.comments: Infections occur mainly during periods of high rainfall and high humidity
Circular.management: Plant trees in area with good sunlight and air circulation; prune trees to an open center; blemishes superficial and can be washed off

Circular.comments: Spores are spread by splashing water.
Circular.management: Remove infected crop debris and burn them. Provide proper air circulation around the plant. If the disease is severe, spray suitable fungicide.

Circular.comments: Disease may be seed-borne.
Circular.management: Use disease free seed; treat seeds prior to planting; rotate crops every 2 years.

Circular.comments: Fungus survives in infected leaves from previous season; disease occurs sporadically in Southeastern U.S. and is common in the U.S. state of Texas
Circular.management: Downy spot is controlled by the fungicides that are applied to protect against scab

Circular.comments: Persea mites are most damaging on Hass and Gwen varieties.
Circular.management: Ensure the tree is adequately fertilized, pruned properly and irrigated to avoid unnecessary stress to the tree which can make them more susceptible to mite attack; persistent infestations may require treatment with an appropriate chemical; organic controls include several types of horticultural oil.

Circular.comments: Close plant spacing favors bacterial spread between plants; bacterium causes severe disease symptoms at temperatures above 30°C (86°F)
Circular.management: Control of disease is difficult and usually involve the application of copper sprays in commercial plantations; some control can be achieved by planting less susceptible celery varieties; mechanical transmission can be reduced by only trimming leaves when foliage is dry

Circular.management: Disease is held in check by fungicides applied to control early or late leaf spot.

Circular.comments: Disease emergence is favored by cool, moist conditions and periods of prolonged leaf wetness.
Circular.management: Remove and destroy infested crop debris to prevent build up of inoculum in soil; rotate crops away from peanut; foliar fungicide applications are largely ineffective if conditions are favorable for the disease.

Circular.management: Spraying with Bordeaux mixture after bud break protects new leaves from becoming infected; avoid over fertilizing vines; fungicide applications are ineffective if disease outbreak is severe

Circular.management: Avoid wounding fruits during harvest

Circular.management: No specific treatments are currently recommended

Circular.comments: Disease emergence favors warm weather; appears early in the spring
Circular.management: Avoid sprinkler irrigation when fruit is ripening; rotate crops with other non-solanaceous plants

Circular.comments: Gray mold is usually associated with wounds on plants; can develop in relatively cool conditions.
Circular.management: Liming soil to increase calcium content can help to reduce plant susceptibility to gray mold; application of appropriate fungicide prior to formation of dense canopy.

Circular.comments: Spread by water splash; fungus overwinters in plant debris.
Circular.management: Ensure all tomato crop debris is removed and destroyed in Fall or plowed deep into soil; plant only disease-free material; avoid overhead irrigation; stake plants to increase air circulation through the foliage; apply appropriate fungicide if necessary.

Circular.comments: Yield losses may result in susceptible varieties due to nuts dropping from tree prematurely; spores are spread by water splash from infected husks; diseased husks may produce spores for up to 2 years following infection
Circular.management: If variety of macadamia being grown is susceptible to the disease then an appropriate protective fungicide should be applied to the fruits when they are approximately the size of a match head; remove and old and diseased husks from the tree to reduce inoculum levels

Circular.comments: Fungus overwinters on leaf debris and on related weeds; disease emergence is favored by moist, warm conditions
Circular.management: Control of disease depends on sanitary practices; treat seeds with hot water prior to planting; rotate crops; plant in an area with good soil drainage; remove all cruciferous weeds which may act as a reservoir for the fungus

Circular.comments: Occurs wherever rice is grown; fungus overwinters on plant debris; disease emergence favored by water on surfaces of plant
Circular.management: Ensure plants are provided with correct nutrients and avoid water stress; chemical seed treatments are effective at reducing the incidence of the disease

Circular.comments: Occurs in all areas where rice is grown; second only importance to rice blast; most damaging in intensive rice production; spreads rapidly via irrigation
Circular.management: Avoid overfertilizing plants as excessive nitrogen application has been shown to increase susceptibility to the disease; applications of foliar fungicides may be required; two applications are recommended and should be timed so that the first application is made between the early internode elongation and the second application made on emerging panicles 10-14 days later

Circular.comments: Favors high humidity but low rainfall; spores can be transported over long distances by wind
Circular.management: No resistance known; use only disease-free seed and plant in well-draining soil; control weeds around crop; apply appropriate protective fungicide
